questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Who among the following is associated with 'Songs from Prison', a translation of ancient Indian religious lyrics in English?
questions.questionText: Who among the following is associated with 'Songs from Prison', a translation of ancient Indian religious lyrics in English?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Mahatma Gandhi** was associated with "**Songs from Prison**", a translation of ancient Indian religious lyrics from Upnishads and other religious scripture, during his imprisionment in Yarvada Jail in 1930s.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: With reference to Indian National Movement, consider the following pairs:
1. Sir Tej Bahadur Sapru - President, All India Liberal Federation
2. K. C. Neogy - Member, The Constituent Assembly
3. P. C. Joshi - General Secretary, Communist Party of India
Which of the pairs given above is/are correctly matched?
questions.questionText: With reference to Indian National Movement, consider the following pairs: 1. Sir Tej Bahadur Sapru - President, All India Liberal Federation 2. K. C. Neogy - Member, The Constituent Assembly 3. P. C. Joshi - General Secretary, Communist Party of India Which of the pairs given above is/are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.

**Pair 1 is correctly matched.**Tej Bahadur Sapru**was the President of**All India Liberal Federation**and was a prominent member of Liberal Party of India.** Sir Tej Bahadur Sapru played a pivotal role in the development of modern India's constitutional and political life.
He was a member of the All India Congress Committee, the General Secretary of the Congress party and also the President of the UP Congress Committee.
He was also a member of the UP Legislative Council, and thereafter of the Imperial Legislative Council. He became one of the founding members of a party that eventually came to be called The National Liberal Federation of India.
He left the Congress in 1919-20**, when the Congress took a turn towards mass politics.**

Pair 2 is correctly matched. **K.C. Neogy** was a member of the **Constituent Assembly of India**, and the member of the first Cabinet of independent India.**Neogy was a member of the Indian National Congress and was elected as a member of the Central Legislative Assembly in**1920**representing Bengal. He was the second Finance Minister of free India. The First Finance Commission was constituted under the chairmanship of KC Neogy for the period 1952-57** in the year **1952**.
**Pair 3 is correctly matched.**P.C. Joshi**was the first General Secretary of the**Communist Party of India**.** He was one of the leading figures of the communist movement in India.
Born in Almora in **1907**. Joshi joined the Communist Party in **1929**, and rose to prominence thereafter. He was arrested in connection with the Meerut Conspiracy Case.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: With reference to Madanapalle of Andhra Pradesh,
which one of the following statements is correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to Madanapalle of Andhra Pradesh, which one of the following statements is correct?

options.a: Pingali Venkayya designed the tricolour Indian National Flag here.
options.b: Pattabhi Sitaramaiah led the Quit India Movement of Andhra region from here.
options.c: Rabindranath Tagore translated the National Anthem from Bengali to English here.
options.d: Madame Blavatsky and Colonel Olcott set up headquarters of Theosophical Society first her

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The **National anthem** was first sung outside the Calcutta by the **Rabndranath Tagore** himself during a session at the Besant Theosophical College in **Madanapalle, Andhra Pradesh**, on 28th Feb **1919**, when Tagore went to college and sang the song.
Then deputy director of the university (also an expert in European music and wife of the Irish poet James Cousins), asked Tagore to create an English translation of the song and establish the musical notation.
Tagore then translated the Bengali song to English and the tune was composed by Margaret with the girls in the college singing the chorus.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: The Vital-Vidhvansak, the first monthly journal to have the untouchable people as its target audience was published by:
questions.questionText: The Vital-Vidhvansak, the first monthly journal to have the untouchable people as its target audience was published by:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The first monthly journal aimed at untouchable people, titled "**Vital-Vidhvansak**" (Destroyer of Brahmanical or Ceremonial Pollution), was published by **Gopal Baba Walangkar** in 1888. His work aimed to raise awareness about the injustices faced by untouchables and to challenge the caste system's position in society, primarily through appealing to the elites and encouraging change.
Walangkar's writings marked an early intellectual rebellion against the caste system. He also wrote articles for Marathi-language newspapers such as Sudharak and Deenbandhu, as well as composing couplets in Marathi that were intended to inspire the people.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: With reference to the book "Desher Katha" written by Sakharam Ganesh Deuskar during the freedom struggle, consider the following statements:
1. It warned against the Colonial State's hypnotic conquest of the mind.
2. It inspired the performance of swadeshi street plays and folk songs.
3. The use of 'desh' by Deuskar was in the specific context of the region of Bengal.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the book "Desher Katha" written by Sakharam Ganesh Deuskar during the freedom struggle, consider the following statements: 1. It warned against the Colonial State's hypnotic conquest of the mind. 2. It inspired the performance of swadeshi street plays and folk songs. 3. The use of 'desh' by Deuskar was in the specific context of the region of Bengal. Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is correct: "**Desher Katha**" by**Sakharam Ganesh Deuskar**warned against the colonial state's influence on the minds of the people.** It highlighted how the colonial state was trying to control and manipulate the thoughts and beliefs of the Indian population.
**Statement 2 is correct: The book "Desher Katha" had a significant impact in Bengal and inspired the performance of**swadeshi (indigenous) street plays and folk songs**.** It played a crucial role in preparing the ground for the Swadeshi movement.
**Statement 3 is incorrect: Sakharam Ganesh Deuskar used the term '**desh**' to refer to the**nation**, not specifically the region of Bengal.** His book had a broader perspective on the exploitation of India as a whole by the colonial state.
**

Important Tips **"Desher Katha" by Sakharam Ganesh Deuskar**: Sakharam Ganesh Deuskar, a Marathi Brahmin who settled in Bengal, was born in Deoghar.
He published the book "Desher Katha" in **1904**, which provided an exhaustive account of British commercial and industrial exploitation in India.
"Desher Katha" sold ten thousand copies in its first year and reached its fifth edition by **1905**. The Bengal government banned the book in **1910** and confiscated all copies.
Deuskar popularized the ideas of Dadabhai Naoroji and M.G. Ranade, promoting Swadeshi in a popular format. His book warned against the colonial state's manipulation of Indian minds, termed as its 'hypnotic conquest.' Despite the growth of regional and national consciousness, Bengali lacked a specific word for 'nation.' Deuskar used 'desh' to refer to the nation, infusing a broader perspective beyond Bengal.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who among the following were the founders of the "Hind Mazdoor Sabha" established in 1948?
questions.questionText: Who among the following were the founders of the "Hind Mazdoor Sabha" established in 1948?

options.a: B. Krishna Pillai, E.M.S. Namboodiripad and K.C. George
options.b: Jayaprakash Narayan, Deen Dayal Upadhyay and M.N. Roy
options.c: C.P. Ramaswamy Iyer, K. Kamaraj and Veeresalingam Pantulu
options.d: Ashok Mehta, T.S. Ramanujam and G.G. Mehta

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Till independence, there was only one central trade union organisation, the All India Trade Union Congress (AITUC) which was formed in **1920** and created lot of industrial strife.
The Congress Party therefore took the initiative in forming a trade union centre (INTUC). The socialists in INTUC were dissatisfied with the functioning of INTUC and founded **Hind Mazdoor Sabha (HMS)** in Howrah as a National Trade Union Centre in India.
Its founders included **Basawan Singh (Sinha), Ashok Mehta, R.S. Ruikar, Mani Benkara, Shibnath Benerajee, T.S. Ramanujam, VS. Mathur, G.G. Mehta**.
Mr. R.S. Ruikar was elected president and Ashok Mehta as its General Secretary. It acted as a balancing force between the INTUC and AITUC.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Consider the following events:
1. The first democratically elected communist party government formed in a State in India.
2. India's then largest bank, 'Imperial Bank of India', was renamed 'State Bank of India'.
3. Air India was nationalised and became the national carrier.
4. Goa became a part of independent India.
Which of the following is the correct chronological sequence of the above events?
questions.questionText: Consider the following events: 1. The first democratically elected communist party government formed in a State in India. 2. India's then largest bank, 'Imperial Bank of India', was renamed 'State Bank of India'. 3. Air India was nationalised and became the national carrier. 4. Goa became a part of independent India. Which of the following is the correct chronological sequence of the above events?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **01-08 -**1953**: Air India was nationalised** under the Air Corporations Act, **1953** and becomes India's national flight.
**01-07 -**1955**: India's largest bank Imperial Bank of India renamed as State Bank of India.**
**05-04 -**1957**: First democratically elected Communist Party government** outside Europe gets to power in Kerala with CPI leader EMS Namboodiripad as the Chief Minister.
**19-12 -**1961**: 19th December is observed as the Goa Liberation Day**. Portuguese rule was ended on this day.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: He wrote biographies of Mazzini, Garibaldi, Shivaji and Shrikrishna; stayed in America for some time; and was also elected to the Central Assembly. He was
questions.questionText: He wrote biographies of Mazzini, Garibaldi, Shivaji and Shrikrishna; stayed in America for some time; and was also elected to the Central Assembly. He was
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Lala Lajpat Rai** was prolific writer and authored several works like "Unhappy India", "Young India: An Interpretation", "History of Arya Samaj", "England's Debt to India".
He also wrote several **biographies of Mazzini (Life of Mazzini), Garibaldi, Shivaji (Chatrapati Shivaji) and Hindu god Shri Krishna (Yogiraj Shrikrishna)**.
His purpose in selecting Mazzini and Garibladi was to infuse patriotic sentiment in the youth of Punjab, who had no access to books in English.
**

Important Tips **Facts related to Lala Lajpat Rai:** In **1917**, Lala Lajpat Rai moved to the United States during the First World War and founded the Indian Home Rule League of America in New York.
He stayed in the United States from **1917** to **1920**. Rai returned to India in **1920** and in the following year led the special session of the Indian Congress Party that launched the non-co-operation movement.
He joined Swaraj Party in **1926** and was elected as its Deputy Leader in the Central Legislative Assembly.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Annie Besant was
1. responsible for starting the Home Rule Movement
2. the founder of the Theosophical Society
3. once the President of the Indian National Congress
Select the correct statement/statements using the codes given below:
questions.questionText: Annie Besant was 1. responsible for starting the Home Rule Movement 2. the founder of the Theosophical Society 3. once the President of the Indian National Congress Select the correct statement/statements using the codes given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is correct.**Annie Besant**along with Bal Gangadhar Tilak had formed and led the**Home Rule Movement (**1916**
- **1918**)**.**
It was based on the lines of Irish Home Rule League aimed at achieving self-government and dominion status within British Empire.

**Statement 2 is incorrect.**Theosophical Society was founded in New York by Madam Blavatsky and Henry Steel Olcott**and was later shifted to Adyar Chennai.** The founders of this organization were inspired by Indian thought and culture.
**Statement 3 is correct. She became the**first woman president of Indian National Congress**when she presisded over the**1917**Calcutta Session.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Mahatma Gandhi said that some of his deepest convictions were reflected in a book titled, "Unto this Last" and the book transformed his life.
What was the message from the book that transformed Mahatma Gandhi?
questions.questionText: Mahatma Gandhi said that some of his deepest convictions were reflected in a book titled, "Unto this Last" and the book transformed his life. What was the message from the book that transformed Mahatma Gandhi?

options.a: Uplifting the oppressed and poor is the moral responsibility of an educated man
options.b: The good of individual is contained in the good of all
options.c: The life of celibacy and spiritual pursuit are essential for a noble life
options.d: All the statements (a), (b) and (c) are correct in this context

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. Mohandas Gandhi translated **Unto This Last** into Gujarati in **1908** under the title of Sarvodaya (Well Being of All).
He summarised Unto This Last's teachings in these three points: That **the good of the individual is contained in the good of all**. That a lawyer's work has the same value as the barber's, in as much as all have the same right of earning their livelihood from their work.
That a life of labour, i.e., the life of the tiller of the soil and the handicraftsman, is the life worth living.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: With reference to Indian freedom struggle, Usha Mehta is well-known for?
questions.questionText: With reference to Indian freedom struggle, Usha Mehta is well-known for?

options.a: Running the secret congress radio in the wake of quit India movement
options.b: Participating in the second round table conference
options.c: Leading a contingent of Indian national army
options.d: Assisting in the formation of Interim Government under Pandit Jawaharlal Nehru

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Usha Mehta** was an active supporter of the Quit India Movement. She started an **underground radio in Bombay**.
She was an important member of a small group which ran the **Congress Radio**. During Quit India, many nationalists went underground and took to subversive activities.
The main personalities taking up underground activity were Rammanohar Lohia, Jayaprakash Narayan, Aruna Asaf Ali, Usha Mehta, Biju Patnaik, Chhotubhai Puranik, Achyut Patwardhan, Sucheta Kripalani and R.P. Goenka.
Underground activities were meant to keep up popular morale by continuing to provide a line of command and guidance to distribute arms and ammunition.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Consider the following statements:
1. Dr. Rajendra Prasad persuaded Mahatma Gandhi to come to Champaran to investigate the problem of peasants.
2. Acharya J.B. Kripalani was one of Mahatma Gandhi's colleagues in his Champaran investigation.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: 1. Dr. Rajendra Prasad persuaded Mahatma Gandhi to come to Champaran to investigate the problem of peasants. 2. Acharya J.B. Kripalani was one of Mahatma Gandhi's colleagues in his Champaran investigation. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is incorrect: It was not Dr. Rajendra Prasad who persuaded Mahatma Gandhi to come to Champaran, but**Raj Kumar Shukla**, a farmer from Champaran who was suffering from the exploitation of the European indigo planters.**

**Statement 2 is correct:**Acharya J.B. Kripalani**was one of Mahatma Gandhi's colleagues in his**Champaran investigation**.** Kripalani was a prominent educator, social activist and politician who was a close associate of Gandhi and a longtime supporter of his ideology.
He joined Gandhi in Champaran along with other eminent lawyers and helped him mobilize the peasants for the Champaran Satyagraha, which was Gandhi's first civil disobedience movement in India.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Four resolutions were passed at the famous Calcutta Session of Indian National Congress in
1906. The question of either retention or rejection of these four resolutions became the cause of a split in Congress at the next Congress Session held in Surat in
Which one of the following was not one of those resolutions?
questions.questionText: Four resolutions were passed at the famous Calcutta Session of Indian National Congress in 1906. The question of either retention or rejection of these four resolutions became the cause of a split in Congress at the next Congress Session held in Surat in 1907. Which one of the following was not one of those resolutions?

options.a: Annulment of the partition of Bengal

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. In **1906**, the session at Calcutta was presided by Dada Bhai Naoroji. The moderates choose Dada Bhai Naoroji to preside over the Congress.
But, in this session, the congress was compelled by the extremists to adopt the following resolutions which were accepted by the moderates with half heart.
**Resolution on National Education. Resolution of Self Government (Swaraj). Resolution on Swadeshi. Resolution on Boycott** In this resolutions, the boycott of British goods was put forward and an impassioned plea was made for cultivation of the nationalist ideals.
King George V at his Coronation Durbar in Delhi in December **1911** announced the revocation of the Partition of Bengal. Hence **Annulment was done in**1911**and not in 1907**. Hence, Option 1 is not one of those resolutions. **

Important Tips Dada Bhai Naoroji presided over the INC session at Calcutta in **1906** in which a resolution supporting the program of swadeshi, boycott, and national education was passed.
Ras Behari Ghosh presided over the Surat session in **1907** in which congress was split into moderates and extremists.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: Who among the following Governor-Generals created the Covenanted Civil Service of India which later came to be known as the Indian Civil Service?
questions.questionText: Who among the following Governor-Generals created the Covenanted Civil Service of India which later came to be known as the Indian Civil Service?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Cornwallis** was the Governor-General of India from 1786 to 1793, and he created the **Covenanted Civil Service of India** as part of his administrative reforms.
He introduced a system of merit-based recruitment and promotion for the civil servants, who were required to sign a covenant of good behavior and loyalty to the Company.
He also raised their salaries and prohibited them from engaging in private trade or accepting gifts from the Indians. He aimed to create a professional and efficient civil service that would serve the interests of the Company and the British Empire.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who of the following is the author of a collection of poems called 'Golden Threshold'?
questions.questionText: Who of the following is the author of a collection of poems called 'Golden Threshold'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. "**The Golden Threshold**" is a collection of poems by **Sarojini Naidu**, who was often referred to as "Bharatiya Kokila" or "The Nightingale of India." She was a prominent Indian poet, freedom fighter, and child prodigy.
Notably, Naidu was the first Indian woman to serve as the President of the Indian National Congress and the first woman to become a governor of an Indian state.
Her poetry, known for its lyrical beauty, was published in this collection in **1905**. **

Important Tips **Notable works of Sarojini Naidu: The Golden Threshold (**1905**)**: Naidu's debut collection of poems that introduced her poetic talent to the world.
**The Bird of Time: Songs of Life, Death & the Spring (**1915**)**: Another collection of Naidu's poetry, exploring themes of life, death, and the changing seasons.
**The Broken Wing: Songs of Love, Death, and the Spring (**1917**)**: A poetic work delving into themes of love, loss, and the renewal of life in spring.
**The Song of the Palanquin Bearers (**1919**)**: Naidu's lyrics set to music by Martin Shaw, reflecting the cultural and musical influences of her time.
**The Sceptred Flute: Songs of India (**1948**)**: A collection of poems showcasing Naidu's deep connection to Indian culture and her poetic prowess.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Who among the following Gandhian followers was a teacher by profession?
questions.questionText: Who among the following Gandhian followers was a teacher by profession?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **J.B. Kripalani** was a **teacher by profession** among the Gandhian followers. He was a professor of English and History at Fergusson College in Pune before joining the freedom movement following Gandhi's return from South Africa.
He came in contact with Gandhi in **1917** during Champaran Satyagraha and became one of his loyal supporters. **

Important Tips **A. N. Sinha:** He was an Indian politician and a leader of the Indian National Congress.
He was also a lawyer, journalist, and social reformer who fought for the rights of the peasants and workers in Bihar. He was known as the "Bihar Kesari" or the Lion of Bihar.
He was a member of the Constituent Assembly of India and served as the first Deputy Chief Minister of Bihar. **Braj Kishore Prasad:** He was an Indian lawyer and a leader of the Indian National Congress.
He was also a freedom fighter who participated in various movements such as the Non-Cooperation Movement, the Civil Disobedience Movement, and the Quit India Movement.
He was a close associate of Rajendra Prasad and served as the President of the Bihar Provincial Congress Committee. He was also a member of the Constituent Assembly of India.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who among the following translated the autobiography of Madam Curie in Hindi?
questions.questionText: Who among the following translated the autobiography of Madam Curie in Hindi?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Lal Bahadur Shastri**, who served as the second Prime Minister of India, **translated the autobiography of Madam Curie into Hindi**.
Madam Curie was a pioneering physicist and chemist known for her groundbreaking research on radioactivity, and her autobiography provides insights into her life and scientific contributions.
Lal Bahadur Shastri's translation made this work accessible to Hindi-speaking readers, contributing to the dissemination of scientific knowledge in India.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who among the following wrote the poem "Subh-e Azadi"?
questions.questionText: Who among the following wrote the poem "Subh-e Azadi"?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The poem "**Subh-e Azadi**" (Dawn of Freedom) was written by the renowned Pakistani poet **Faiz Ahmed Faiz** in August **1947**. This Urdu poem reflects the poet's sentiments about the partition of India and Pakistan, expressing disappointment, anguish, and a critical atmosphere surrounding the events.
Faiz's poem sympathizes with those who migrated during the partition and addresses the pain and sadness associated with the cost of sovereignty.
It has been both praised and criticized for its views and ideological style regarding the partition. **

Important Tips **Faiz Ahmed Faiz:** Faiz Ahmed Faiz, a prominent poet of the 20th century, crafted verses that transcend time.
His poems are a blend of emotions, weaving themes of love, resistance, and social justice. Some of his notable poems: "Aaj Bazaar Mein Pa-Ba Joulaan Chalo" exemplifies his metaphoric brilliance in depicting the struggle against oppression.
"Aaj Ik Harf Ko Phir" portrays his fiery desire to express. "Bahaar Aayi" explores love tinged with sorrow. "Bol" champions raising voices against injustice.
"Kab Yaad Mein Tera Saath Nahin" and "Mujhse Pehli Si Muhabbat Mehboob Na Maang" delve into lost love.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Elizabeth Hawley is well-known for her writings relating to
which one of the following?
questions.questionText: Elizabeth Hawley is well-known for her writings relating to which one of the following?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Elizabeth Hawley** is renowned for her meticulous chronicling of **Himalayan expeditions**.
Although she never climbed mountains herself, her Himalayan Database serves as a comprehensive and trusted record of climbs in the Nepalese Himalaya.
She was respected for her accuracy and investigative skills, earning her the nickname "The Sherlock Holmes of the Mountaineering World." Hawley's work was crucial for climbers, establishing success and fatality rates and settling disputes over summit claims.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who, among the following, wrote the book 'Bahubivah'?
questions.questionText: Who, among the following, wrote the book 'Bahubivah'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The book "**Bahubivah**" was written by **Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar** in 1871. Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar was a renowned social reformer and educator in 19th-century India.
He advocated for various social reforms, including widow remarriage and women's education. "Bahubivah" was one of his works that aimed to address the issue of polygamy, particularly among the Hindu community, and promote social change to improve women's rights and status.
**

Important Tips **Some of the literary works of Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar:** Betaal Panchavinsati (1847): A collection of folktales Banglar Itihaas (1848): A history of Bengal Jivancharita (1849): A biography of Raja Rammohan Roy Bodhadoy (1851): A book on ethics Upakramanika (1851): A book on Sanskrit grammar Rijupath (**1851-52**): A book on logic Byakaran Kaumudi (1853): A book on Bengali grammar Borno Parichoy (1854): A book on Bengali phonetics Shakuntala (1854): A translation of the Sanskrit play Shakuntala Mahabharata (1860): A translation of the Sanskrit epic Mahabharata Seetar Vanavas (1860): A translation of the Sanskrit epic Ramayana Ratnopariksha (1886): A book on philosophy Bidhobabivah (1855), concerning the rights of widows to remarry.
Bahubivah (1871) against polygamy. Balyabivah (1871) against child marriage.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: The song Amar Sonar Bangla' written during the Swadeshi Movement of India inspired the liberation struggle of Bangladesh and was adopted as the National Anthem of Bangladesh.
Who wrote this song?
questions.questionText: The song Amar Sonar Bangla' written during the Swadeshi Movement of India inspired the liberation struggle of Bangladesh and was adopted as the National Anthem of Bangladesh. Who wrote this song?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The song "**Amar Sonar Bangla**" was written by **Rabindranath Tagore** in **1905** during the first partition of Bengal, which occurred under British rule.
The song served as an ode to Mother Bengal and was intended to rekindle the spirit of unity in Bengal amidst communal divisions. In **1971**, during the liberation war of Bangladesh, the first ten lines of this song were adopted as the national anthem of Bangladesh, symbolizing its struggle for independence and the nation's identity.
**

Important Tips **Rabindranath Tagore Literary works:** His poetic journey encompasses several remarkable collections, including "Manasi" (1890), known as "The Ideal One," and "Sonar Tari" (1894), translated as "The Golden Boat." However, it was "Gitanjali" (**1910**), or "Song Offerings," that catapulted him to international acclaim, earning him the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Tagore continued to enchant readers with "Gitimalya" (**1914**) and "Balaka" (**1916**), commonly referred to as "The Flight of Cranes." Tagore's literary legacy extends into the realm of drama, with notable plays like "Raja" (**1910**), exploring themes of love and darkness, and "Dakghar" (**1912**), which poignantly captures the imagination of a young boy confined to his home.
"Achalayatan" (**1912**) delves into the human spirit's quest for freedom, while "Muktadhara" (**1922**) addresses societal reform. "Raktakaravi" (**1926**), or "Red Oleanders," confronts themes of oppression and rebellion. In addition to his dramatic works, Tagore ventured into prose and novels.
"Gora" (**1910**) grapples with identity, social reform, and nationalism, while "Ghare-Baire" (**1916**), known as "The Home and the World," delves into the complexities of love, politics, and personal ideals.
"Yogayog" (**1929**), or "Crosscurrents," explores the clash between tradition and modernity. Beyond fiction, Tagore crafted an extensive body of work, including short stories, essays, travel diaries, and autobiographies.
His creative talents extended to musical and dance dramas, and he was an accomplished artist, creating drawings and paintings.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Consider the following statements:
1. Jawaharlal Nehru was in his fourth term as the Prime Minister of India at the time of his death.
2. Jawaharlal Nehru represented Rae Bareilly constituency as a Member of Parliament.
3. The first non-Congress Prime Minister of India assumed the office in the year 1977.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: 1. Jawaharlal Nehru was in his fourth term as the Prime Minister of India at the time of his death. 2. Jawaharlal Nehru represented Rae Bareilly constituency as a Member of Parliament. 3. The first non-Congress Prime Minister of India assumed the office in the year 1977. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is correct:**Jawaharlal Nehru**served as the Prime Minister of India from August 15,**1947**, when India gained independence, until his death on May 27,**1964**.**He was in his 4th term when he died**, (1947-51**, 1952-57**,**1957**-**1962**, 1962-64**)**

Statement 2 is incorrect: Jawaharlal Nehru **represented the Phulpur constituency** in the Lok Sabha (lower house of Parliament).**

Statement 3 is correct: The **first non-Congress Prime Minister of India was Morarji Desai**, who assumed office in **1977** after Janata alliance won the general elections.**This marked a significant political shift in India as the Indian National Congress had been in power since independence in**1947**.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who wrote the book "The Story of The Integration of the Indian States"?
questions.questionText: Who wrote the book "The Story of The Integration of the Indian States"?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The book, "**The Story of the Integration of the Indian States**," is authored by **V.P Menon**.
It recounts the complex process of integrating princely states into India after independence, highlighting the collective efforts and challenges faced during the period of **1947**
- **1951**.
The author provides an objective account of historical events, emphasizing the role of unsung heroes in this significant consolidation of the nation.
The book also sheds light on the background of the princely states and the intricacies of this monumental task.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: Consider the following statements:
1. Robert Clive was the first Governor-General of Bengal.
2. William Bentinck was the first Governor-General of India.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: 1. Robert Clive was the first Governor-General of Bengal. 2. William Bentinck was the first Governor-General of India. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is incorrect: The**first Governor-General of Bengal was Warren Hastings**, who served from 1773 to 1785. Robert Clive was not the first Governor-General of Bengal.** Robert Clive served as Governor of Bengal twice, from 1757 to 1760 and from 1764 to 1767. However, he was not the first Governor-General of Bengal, which was a higher position created by the Regulating Act of 1773 to oversee the affairs of the East India Company in India.
**Statement 2 is correct:**William Bentinck was the first Governor-General of India**, which was a title given to him by the Charter Act of 1833 that abolished the East India Company's monopoly on trade and gave more power to the British Crown in India.** He served as Governor-General of India from 1834 to 1835, after being Governor-General of the Presidency of Fort William (Bengal) from 1828 to 1834. He is credited with reforms in educational, social and judicial spheres during his tenure as Governor-General of British India.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who has written the book named 'Gita Rahasya'?
questions.questionText: Who has written the book named 'Gita Rahasya'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The book '**Gita Rahasya**' was written by **Bal Gangadhar Tilak**, an Indian social reformer and independence activist.
This Marathi language book, written while Tilak was in prison in Mandalay, Burma, in **1915**, analyzes Karma yoga as found in the Bhagavad Gita.
Tilak emphasized the message of Nishkam Karmayoga (selfless action) from the Gita. The book comprises philosophical exposition, the Gita, its translation, and commentary.
**

Important Tips **Literary works of Bal Gangadhar Tilak:** Tilak founded two influential newspapers, Kesari in Marathi and Mahratta in English in **1880-18**81.
Kesari later became a daily newspaper and is still published today. In **1903**, Tilak authored "The Arctic Home in the Vedas," positing that the Vedas originated in the Arctic region and were brought south by Aryan bards post the last ice age.
In "The Orion," he attempted to date the Vedas by studying the positions of Nakshatras.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who among the following was a proponent of Fabianism as a movement?
questions.questionText: Who among the following was a proponent of Fabianism as a movement?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Annie Besant** was a proponent of **Fabianism** as a movement. Annie Besant was a prominent member of the Fabian Society and actively promoted the ideas of Fabianism in her writings and political activities.
**

Important Tips **Fabianism:** It was founded in 1884, is a form of socialism that advances the principles of democratic framework to achieve gradual conversion to socialism rather than by revolutionary overthrow.
It is a type of socialism through peaceful and constitutional method with the help of middle class. They believe in the authority of State. State is an important agent to bring socialism.
Eg. Labour party in Britain is one of outcome of ideology of Fabianism. After Independence, Nehru was the supporter of Fabianism.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: Consider the following Viceroys of India during the British rule:
1. Lord Curzon
2. Lord Hardinge
3. Lord Chelmsford
4. Lord Irwin
Which one of the following is the correct chronological order of their tenure?
questions.questionText: Consider the following Viceroys of India during the British rule: 1. Lord Curzon 2. Lord Hardinge 3. Lord Chelmsford 4. Lord Irwin Which one of the following is the correct chronological order of their tenure?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The correct chronological order of the tenure of the four Viceroys of India during the British rule is:
**Lord Curzon** was the Viceroy of India from **1899 to 1905**. He was known for his imperialist and controversial policies, such as the partition of Bengal, the Indian Universities Act, the Curzon-Kitchener controversy, and the Delhi Durbar.
**Lord Hardinge** was the Viceroy of India from **1910 to 1916**. He reversed the partition of Bengal in **1911**. He also shifted the capital of India from Calcutta to Delhi in **1911**. He faced the challenge of the First World War and its impact on India.
**Lord Chelmsford** was the Viceroy of India from **1916 to 1921**. He introduced the Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms in **1919**, which granted more autonomy and representation to Indians in provincial legislatures.
He also faced the aftermath of the Jallianwala Bagh massacre in **1919**, which sparked widespread protests and violence across India. **Lord Irwin** was the Viceroy of India from **1926 to 1931**. He initiated the Simon Commission in **1927**, which was boycotted by all Indian political parties.
He signed the Gandhi-Irwin Pact in **1931**, which ended the civil disobedience movement and agreed to send a delegation to the Second Round Table Conference in London.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: Which one of the following pairs is not correctly matched?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following pairs is not correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Vernacular Press Act: Curzon is not correctly matched**, because the Vernacular Press Act was passed by **Lord Lytton**, not Lord Curzon.
The Vernacular Press Act was a law enacted in 1878 to regulate the publication of newspapers and books in Indian languages. The Act gave the British government the power to censor and suppress any content that was deemed seditious or offensive to the British rule.
The Act was widely criticized by the Indian nationalists and journalists, who saw it as a violation of their freedom of expression and a sign of racial discrimination.
**

Important Tips **Pitt's India Act** was passed in 1784 during the tenure of **Warren Hastings** as the Governor-General of Bengal. The Act was intended to address the shortcomings of the Regulating Act of 1773 by bringing the East India Company's rule in India under the control of the British government.
**Doctrine of Lapse** was an annexation policy followed by **Lord Dalhousie** when he was the Governor-General of India from 1848 to 1856. According to this policy, any princely state under the direct or indirect control of the East India Company where the ruler did not have a legal male heir would be annexed by the Company.
**Ilbert Bill** was a proposed law introduced by **Lord Ripon** when he was the Viceroy of India from 1880 to 1884. The Bill aimed to remove the racial discrimination in the judicial system by allowing Indian judges and magistrates to try cases involving British subjects in India.
The Bill was eventually modified and passed in 1884, but it failed to satisfy either the Indians or the British.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: Consider the following Princely States of the British rule in India:
1. Jhansi
2. Sambalpur
3. Satara The correct chronological order in which they were annexed by the British is:
questions.questionText: Consider the following Princely States of the British rule in India: 1. Jhansi 2. Sambalpur 3. Satara The correct chronological order in which they were annexed by the British is:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Satara** was the first princely state to be annexed by the British in **1848** using the Doctrine of Lapse, followed by **Sambalpur** in **1849** and **Jhansi** in **1853**. The Doctrine of Lapse was a policy devised by Lord Dalhousie, the governor-general of India from 1848 to 1856, which stated that if a ruler of a dependent state died without a natural heir, the state would be annexed by the British.
This policy was widely resented by the Indian princes and contributed to the outbreak of the Indian Rebellion of 1857.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which one of the following statements is not correct?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements is not correct?

options.a: The National Song Vande Mataram was composed by Bankim Chandra Chatterji originally in Bengali.
options.b: The National Calendar of India based on Saka era has its 1st Chaitra on 22nd March Normally & 21st March in a leap year.
options.c: The design of the National Flag of India was adopted by the Constituent Assembly on 22nd July 1947.
options.d: The song Jana-Gana-Mana, composed originally in Bengali by Rabindranath Tagore was adopted in its Hindi version by the Constituent Assembly on 24 January 1950 as the national anthem.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Vande Mataram** is a poem written in **Sanskrit and Sanskritised Bengali** by Bankim Chandra Chatterjee in the 1870s.
The first two verses of the poem were adopted as the National Song of India in October **1937** by the Congress. The poem was first published in 1882 as part of Chatterjee's Bengali novel Anandmath.
It is an ode to the motherland, personified as the "mother goddess" in later verses, of the people. **

Important Tips The **National Calendar of India**, following the Saka era, marks its first day of Chaitra on March 22 in regular years and on March 21 in leap years.
This system was officially adopted on March 22, **1957**, alongside the Gregorian calendar, for official purposes including Gazette publications, All India Radio broadcasts, Government-issued calendars, and public communications.
The **national flag of India** is a horizontal rectangular tricolour flag, the colours being of India saffron, white and India green; with the Ashoka Chakra, a 24-spoke wheel, in navy blue at its centre. It was adopted in its present form during a meeting of the Constituent Assembly held on 22 July **1947**, and it became the official flag of the Union of India on 15 August **1947**. The National Flag of India was designed by Pingali Venkayya in **1921**.
"**Jana Gana Mana**" is the national anthem of the Republic of India. It was originally composed as Bharoto Bhagyo Bidhata in Bengali by Rabindranath Tagore on 11 December **1911**. The first stanza of the song Bharoto Bhagyo Bidhata was adopted by the Constituent Assembly of India as the National Anthem on 24 January **1950**. A formal rendition of the national anthem takes approximately 52 seconds.
It was first publicly sung on 27 December **1911** at the Calcutta Session of the Indian National Congress.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: Match list-I with list-II and
select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists:
1. A. Macdonald -
1. Doctrine of Lapse
2. B. Linlithgow -
2. Communal Award 3.
C. Dalhousie -
3. August Offer
4. D. Chelmsford -
4. Dyarchy Code:
questions.questionText: Match list-I with list-II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists: 1. A. Macdonald - 1. Doctrine of Lapse 2. B. Linlithgow - 2. Communal Award 3. C. Dalhousie - 3. August Offer 4. D. Chelmsford - 4. Dyarchy Code:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Option A in List-I matches Option 2 in List-II: The **Communal Award** was created by the British prime minister **Ramsay MacDonald** on 16 August **1932**. Also known as the MacDonald Award, it was announced after the Round Table Conference (1930-32**) and extended the separate electorate to depressed Classes (now known as the Scheduled Caste) and other minorities. Option B in List-I matches Option 3 in List-II: The**August Offer**was an offer made by Viceroy**Linlithgow**in**1940**promising the expansion of the Executive Council of the Viceroy of India to include more Indians, the establishment of an advisory war council, giving full weight to minority opinion, and the recognition of Indians' right to frame their own constitution. Option C in List-I matches Option 1 in List-II: The**Doctrine of Lapse**was introduced by Lord**Dalhousie**. According to this doctrine, if any Indian ruler dies without leaving a male heir, his kingdom will automatically pass over to the British. Option D in List-I matches Option 4 in List-II:**Dyarchy**was introduced as a constitutional reform by Edwin Samuel Montagu (secretary of state for India, 1917-22**) and Lord **Chelmsford** (viceroy of India, 1916-21**).
The principle of dyarchy was a division of the executive branch of each provincial government into authoritarian and popularly responsible sections.
The first was composed of executive Councilors, appointed, as before, by the crown. The second was composed of ministers who were chosen by the governor from the elected members of the provincial legislature.
These latter ministers were Indians.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: With reference to the Indian freedom struggle,
which one of the following statements is not correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Indian freedom struggle, which one of the following statements is not correct?

options.a: Hakim Ajmal Khan was one of the leaders to start a nationalist and militant Ahrar movement.
options.b: When the Indian National Congress was formed, Sayyid Ahmad Khan opposed it.
options.c: The All-India Muslim League which was formed in 1906 vehemently opposed the partition of Bengal and separate electorates.
options.d: Maulana Barkat Ullah and Maulana Obeidullah Sindhi were among those who formed a Provisional Government of India in Kabul.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Sir syed Ahmed Khan** felt that the Indian National Congress did not work towards the uplifment of the Indian Muslim community.
Therefore, he opposed the Indian National Congress. Hence Statement b is correct. A group of Muslim landlords and nawabs formed the **All India Muslim League** at Dacca in **1906**. **The League supported the partition of Bengal.** It desired separate electorates for Muslims, a demand conceded by the government in **1909**. Hence Statement c is not correct.
The **Ahrar Movement** was founded in **1910** under the leadership of Maulana Muhammad Ali Mohamed and **Hakim Ajmal Khan**. Other leaders of the movement were Iqbal, CR Das, Sir Mian Muhammad Shafi etc. These leaders were strictly against the Lucknow pact, which favoured the representation of religious minorities in provincial legislatures.
This weightage formula of the Lucknow pact paved the way for the Muslim majority by the late 1920s. Hence Statement a is correct. The first **Provisional Government of India** was established in Kabul during First World War in December **1915**. It was the government-in-exile of Free Hindustan with Raja Mahendra Pratap as president, **Maulana Barkatullah**, Prime Minister, **Maulana Ubaidullah Sindhi**, Home Minister.
Hence Statement d is correct.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who among the following was the President of the All-India States Peoples Conference in 1939?
questions.questionText: Who among the following was the President of the All-India States Peoples Conference in 1939?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The **1939** Session of **All India States People's Conference** was held at Ludhiana, Punjab under the leadership of **Jawaharlal Nehru**.
Jawaharlal Nehru continued to hold this position till **1946**. In his presidential speech to the Conference, titled "Freedom is Indivisible", he denounced the political backwardness and oppression inflicted on the people.
He also asserted the INC's commitment to a united and free India, and criticized those who dreamt of political independence for their states separate from India.
The Ludhiana Session of the All India States Peoples' Conference was a milestone in the growth of the freedom movement, and signaled the unification of the different strands of nationalism that had developed **

Important Tips The **All India States Peoples' Conference (AISPC)** was the association of the Praja Mandals in the princely states under the British Raj.
These Praja Mandals, established all over the country sought to introduce responsible government. The first session of AISPC was held in Bombay in **1927** which brought together representatives from various Indian princely states.
In **1939**, The Indian National Congress amended its policy to lend support to the AISPC.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: A London branch of the All-India Muslim League was published in 1908 under the presidency of:
questions.questionText: A London branch of the All-India Muslim League was published in 1908 under the presidency of:
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The **London branch of the All India Muslim League** was established in **1908** under the presidency of "**Syed Ameer Ali**" to put pressure on British Government for Muslims cause.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: At the time of Partition of India,
which one of the following provinces of British India came forward with a plan for a United and Independent existence?
questions.questionText: At the time of Partition of India, which one of the following provinces of British India came forward with a plan for a United and Independent existence?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Only Punjab in British India proposed a united and independent existence** during partition, led by Sikh leader Master Tara Singh.
Congress sought Punjab's unity with India, while the Muslim League aimed for a separate Muslim-majority state. Ultimately, Punjab was religiously partitioned. Other provinces, like Assam, Bengal, and Bihar, joined India without such plans.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: The native State of Tripura became involved in the Freedom movement early in the 20th century because:
questions.questionText: The native State of Tripura became involved in the Freedom movement early in the 20th century because:

options.a: The kings of Tripura were always anti-British
options.b: The Bengal revolutionaries took shelter in Tripura
options.c: The tribes of the state were fiercely freedom loving
options.d: There were already some groups fighting against the kingship and its protector, the British

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The native State of Tripura became involved in the Freedom movement early in the 20th century because **there were already some groups fighting against the kingship and its protector, the British**.
Raja Bir Bikram Manikya's government was under British influence, and the oppression and exploitation faced by tribes, like the Reangs, led to uprisings against both the local rulers and the British.
This involvement reflected a broader discontent with colonial rule and its collaborators.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: The last major extension of British Indian territory took place during the time of:
questions.questionText: The last major extension of British Indian territory took place during the time of:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Lord Dalhousie** was the Governor-General of India from 1848 to 1856, and he was responsible for the **last major extension of British Indian territory**.
He annexed many Indian states using the Doctrine of Lapse, which stated that if a ruler of a dependent state died without a natural heir, the state would be annexed by the British.
Some of the states that were annexed by this policy were Jhansi, Satara, Jaitpur, Sambalpur, Udaipur, and Nagpur. He also annexed Awadh on the basis of misgovernance, accusing the Nawab of neglecting his subjects and failing to pay his debts to the British.
Lord Dalhousie's aggressive expansionism and interference in the affairs of the Indian princes provoked resentment and resistance among the Indians, and contributed to the outbreak of the Indian Rebellion of 1857, which was also known as the First War of Independence or the Sepoy Mutiny.

Important Tips "**The Indian War of Independence**" is an Indian nationalist history of the 1857 revolt by Vinayak Damodar Savarkar, first published in **1909**. The book was banned in British India due to its inflammatory content but was printed in the Netherlands in **1909**. It describes the 1857 rebellion as a unified Indian uprising against British rule and emphasizes Hindu-Muslim unity.
Savarkar justifies Indian acts of vengeance as reactions to British oppression. "**Sadhana: The Realization of Life**" is a collection of spiritual discourses by Rabindranath Tagore.
It delves into profound questions about existence, God, and the purpose of life. Tagore's lucid explanations draw from Upanishads, Lord Jesus, and Buddha's teachings, making it a valuable spiritual guide.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: At a time when empires in Europe were crumbling before the might of Napoleon,
which one of the following Governors-General kept the British flag flying high in India?
questions.questionText: At a time when empires in Europe were crumbling before the might of Napoleon, which one of the following Governors-General kept the British flag flying high in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Lord Wellesley** was the Governor-General of India from 1798 to 1805, and he **kept the British flag flying high in India** at a time when empires in Europe were crumbling before the might of Napoleon.
He pursued an aggressive policy of expansion and consolidation of British power in India, which earned him the nickname of "the Indian Napoleon".
He implemented the system of subsidiary alliances, by which he forced the Indian rulers to accept British protection and supremacy in exchange for their autonomy and sovereignty.
He also fought several wars against the enemies of the British, such as the Marathas, the Mysoreans, and the French. He also promoted the cultural and educational development of India, by founding the Fort William College and supporting the Asiatic Society of Bengal.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: The Governor-General who followed a spirited "Forward" policy towards Afghanistan was:
questions.questionText: The Governor-General who followed a spirited "Forward" policy towards Afghanistan was:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The '**Forward' policy of Lord Lytton towards Afghanistan** was a misguided attempt to counter the perceived Russian threat in Central Asia.
The policy was based on the assumption that the British should pursue an aggressive policy towards Afghanistan in order to prevent the Russians from gaining influence or territory there.
The policy involved imposing a British resident and troops in Kabul, demanding concessions from the Afghan ruler, and provoking a war when the Afghans resisted.
The policy resulted in the Second Anglo-Afghan War in 1878, which ended in a stalemate and a costly retreat for the British. The policy also alienated the Afghan people, who saw the British as invaders and oppressors. **

Important Tips **Minto** was the Governor-General of India from 1807 to 1813, and he followed a policy of non-intervention and conciliation towards Afghanistan.
He signed a treaty with Shah Shuja, the exiled Afghan ruler, in 1809, which recognized his claim to the throne and promised him British support in case of foreign aggression.
**Dufferin** was the Viceroy of India from 1884 to 1888, and he followed a policy of masterly inactivity towards Afghanistan. He respected the autonomy and sovereignty of Afghanistan and maintained friendly relations with its ruler, Abdur Rahman Khan.
He also settled the boundary dispute between Afghanistan and Russia through diplomatic negotiations. **Elgin** was the Viceroy of India from 1894 to 1899, and he followed a policy of cautious engagement towards Afghanistan.
He supported the reforms and modernization efforts of Abdur Rahman Khan and his successor, Habibullah Khan. He also tried to balance the interests of Britain and Russia in Central Asia through mutual agreements.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: What is the correct sequence of the following events?
1. Tilak's Home Rule League
2. Kamagatamaru Incident
3. Mahatma Gandhi's arrival in India
Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
questions.questionText: What is the correct sequence of the following events? 1. Tilak's Home Rule League 2. Kamagatamaru Incident 3. Mahatma Gandhi's arrival in India Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The correct sequence of events is: **Kamagatamaru Incident (**1914**):** The Komagata Maru incident in **1914** involved the Japanese steamship carrying 376 passengers, primarily Sikhs, Muslims, and Hindus from British India, attempting to immigrate to Canada.
Most were denied entry, leading to a riot and police firing in Calcutta, resulting in 22 deaths. **Mahatma Gandhi's arrival in India (**1915**):** On 9 January **1915**, M K Gandhi reached Bombay sailing from South Africa, having lived there for more than two decades.
This date was chosen in **2003** to be observed as Pravasi Bharatiya Divas (Non-Resident Indian Day). **Tilak's Home Rule League (**1916**):** In **1916**, Tilak established the Indian Home Rule League with headquarters in Poona, primarily active in Maharashtra, Karnataka, Central Provinces, and Berar.
Demands included swarajya, linguistic states, and vernacular education. **

Important Tips **The Komagata Maru incident of**1914**:** The Komagata Maru incident of **1914** involved the Komagata Maru, a passenger ship converted from a coal-transport steamship by businessman Gurdit Singh in Hong Kong.
It arrived in Vancouver with 376 passengers, mostly Sikhs, after setting sail from Hong Kong. Canada's stringent immigration policies required immigrants to arrive via a continuous journey from their country of birth or citizenship.
This aimed to restrict Indian immigration, as many sought works in Canada after traveling through other countries. To advocate for passengers' rights, a Shore Committee formed in Vancouver, led by figures like Hussain Rahim, Sohan Lal Pathak, and Balwant Singh.
In the USA, a campaign led by Barkatullah, Bhagwan Singh, Ram Chandra, and Sohan Singh Bhakna supported the cause. Despite these efforts, the ship was denied entry and forced out of Canadian waters.
After a two-month standoff, the ship was escorted back to India and arrived at Budge Budge, Calcutta. British authorities viewed passengers as lawbreakers and political agitators. Upon arrival, British forces stopped the ship, leading to a clash resulting in the deaths of 18 passengers and the imprisonment of others.
This fueled discontent in Punjab and led to political dacoities in districts like Jalandhar, Amritsar, and Ludhiana. The incident exposed disparities in Canadian immigration laws and galvanized support for the Ghadar Party's revolutionary aims.
Ghadarites like Barkatullah and Tarak Nath Das used it to rally members for the Ghadar movement and plan uprisings in India. Human rights violations highlighted the true face of British rule.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which of the following pairs are correctly matched?
1. Theodore Beck: Mohammadan Anglo-Oriental College, Aligarh
2. Ilbert Bill: Ripon
3. Ferozshah Mehta: Indian National Congress
4. Badruddin Tayabji: Muslims League
Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
questions.questionText: Which of the following pairs are correctly matched? 1. Theodore Beck: Mohammadan Anglo-Oriental College, Aligarh 2. Ilbert Bill: Ripon 3. Ferozshah Mehta: Indian National Congress 4. Badruddin Tayabji: Muslims League Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.

**Pair 1 is correctly matched:**Theodore Beck**was a Quaker and British educationalist working for the British Raj in India.** From 1883 until his death in 1899 he was Principal of **Muhammadan Anglo-Oriental College**, which later became Aligarh Muslim University.
**Pair 2 is correctly matched: The**Ilbert Bill**was a legislative act introduced in 1883 during the tenure of Viceroy**Ripon**and written by Sir Courtenay Pergine Ilbert.** The act stipulated that Indian judges could try Europeans.
**Pair 3 is correctly matched:**Sir Pherozeshah Merwanjee Mehta**was an Indian politician and lawyer from Bombay.** Mehta was one of the founding members and President of the **Indian National Congress** in 1890 held at Calcutta.
He was knighted by the British Government in India for his service to the law. He became the Municipal commissioner of Bombay Municipality in 1873 and its president four times 1884, 1885, **1905** and **1911**.
**Pair 4 is incorrectly matched:**Badruddin Tyabji**was an Indian lawyer, activist, and politician during the British Raj.** Tyabji was the first Indian to practice as a barrister of the High Court of Bombay who served as the third President of the Indian National Congress.
He was one of the founding members and first Muslim president of Indian National Congress. He founded the Anjuman-i-Islam College in Bombay in 1874.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Match list-I with list-II and
select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists:
1. A. Surat Split -
2. B. Communal Award -
C. All Party Convention -
4. D. Poorna Swaraj Resolution -
questions.questionText: Match list-I with list-II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists: 1. A. Surat Split - 1. 1929 2. B. Communal Award - 2. 1928 3. C. All Party Convention - 3. 1932 4. D. Poorna Swaraj Resolution - 4. 1907 5. 1905 Code:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. Option A in List-I matches Option 4 in List-II: The **Surat Split of 1907** divided the Indian National Congress (INC) into Moderates and Radicals.
It resulted from differing approaches to dealing with British rule, with Moderates favoring peaceful negotiations and Radicals advocating more aggressive methods.
This division deepened tensions between the two groups and led to significant political developments in India's struggle for independence. Option B in List-I matches Option 3 in List-II: The **Communal Award**, instituted by British Prime Minister Ramsay MacDonald in **August 1932**, extended separate electorates to depressed classes (Scheduled Castes) and other minorities, building upon the existing system introduced in **1909** and **1919**.
Option C in List-I matches Option 2 in List-II: The **All-Parties Conference** was a group of Indian political parties known for organizing a committee in **May 1928** to author the Constitution of India after independence was actualized.
It was chaired by Dr. M. A. Ansari. Option D in List-I matches Option 1 in List-II: The Indian National Congress, on 19 December **1929**, passed the historic '**Purna Swaraj**' (total independence) resolution at its Lahore session.
A public declaration was made on 26 January **1930**
- a day which the Congress Party urged Indians to celebrate as 'Independence Day'.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: "A graduate at 18, professor and associate editor of the Sudharak at 20, Secretary of the Sarvajanik Sabha and of the Provincial Conference at 25, Secretary of the National Congress at 29, leading witness before an important Royal Commission at 31, Provincial legislator at 34, Imperial legislator at 36, President of the Indian National Congress at
39. a patriot whom Mahatma Gandhi himself regarded as his master".
This is how a biographer describes:
questions.questionText: "A graduate at 18, professor and associate editor of the Sudharak at 20, Secretary of the Sarvajanik Sabha and of the Provincial Conference at 25, Secretary of the National Congress at 29, leading witness before an important Royal Commission at 31, Provincial legislator at 34, Imperial legislator at 36, President of the Indian National Congress at 39. a patriot whom Mahatma Gandhi himself regarded as his master". This is how a biographer describes:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Born on 9 May, 1866, in Maharashtra, **Gopal Krishna Gokhale was an Indian liberal political leader** and a social reformer during the Indian Independence Movement.
He was the political master of Mahatma Gandhi. He was graduate at the age of 18, He became professor and associate editor of the Sudharak at the age 20, He became Secretary of the Sarvajanik Sabha and of the Provincial Conference at the age of 25, He was Secretary of the National Congress at the age of 29, He was leading witness before an important Royal Commission at the age of 31, He was Provincial legislator at the age of 34 and Imperial legislator at age of 36, He was President of the Indian National Congress at age of 39.
**

Important Tips **Gopal Krishna Gokhale** He was the president of INC in **1905** and gave the slogan of "Swadeshi". He founded the "Servants of India Society" in **1905** with the help of Mahadev Govind Ranade.
In addition to learning English, he was exposed to Western political thought and became a great admirer of theorists such as John Stuart Mill and Edmund Burke.
He had a great influence of the social works of Justice Mahadev Govind Ranade on his life. He was named as the Protege Son' i.e. Manas Putra of Justice Mahadev Govind Ranade.
Gokhale's deposition before the Welby Commission on the financial condition of India won him accolades. He played a leading role in bringing about Morley-Minto Reforms in India.

statementLines: Who among the following was associated with suppression of thugs?
questions.questionText: Who among the following was associated with suppression of thugs?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Captain Sleeman was the British officer who was mainly responsible for the suppression of the Thugs**, a notorious gang of robbers and murderers who operated in various parts of India.
He was appointed as the superintendent of the Thuggee and Dacoity Department in 1835 by Lord William Bentinck, the Governor-General of India.
He conducted a systematic campaign to eradicate the Thugs, by using informers, spies, and confessions to identify and arrest them. He also introduced a system of rewards and incentives for the Thugs who turned into witnesses and provided information about their fellow gang members.
He captured and convicted more than 1400 Thugs, who were either hanged or imprisoned for life. He also established a special prison for the Thugs at Jabalpur, where they were reformed and rehabilitated.

Formation of the All-India State People Conference 3. C. 1927 - 3. Foundation of Muslim League at Dhaka 4. D. 1932 - 4. First session of National Conference at Kolkata Code:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Option A in List-I matches Option 4 in List-II: The first **Indian National Union session** was held in Kolkata at Albert Hall from 28 to 30 December **1883**. It was prompted by the Introduction of the Criminal Procedure Amendment Bill (**1883-18**84) or Ilbert Bill.
Option B in List-I matches Option 3 in List-II: The **All-India Muslim League** (AIML) was a political party established in Dhaka in **1906** when some well-known Muslim politicians met the Viceroy of British India, Lord Minto, with the goal of securing Muslim interests on the Indian subcontinent.
Option C in List-I matches Option 2 in List-II: The **All-India States Peoples' Conference** (AISPC) was a conglomeration of political movements in the princely states of the British Raj, which were variously called Praja Mandals or Lok Parishads.
The first session of the organisation was held in Bombay in December **1927**. Option D in List-I matches Option 1 in List-II: British Prime Minister Ramsay MacDonald announced the **Communal Award** on August 16, **1932**, following the recommendations of the Indian Franchise Committee (Lothian Committee).
It aimed to establish separate electorates in British India for various religious and social groups, including Forward Caste, Lower Caste, Muslims, Buddhists, Sikhs, Indian Christians, Anglo-Indians, Europeans, and Untouchables (now known as the Dalits).
**

Important Tips **Key Highlights of Indian Franchise Committee (Lothian Committee): Primary Aim:** To establish a suitable foundation for a system of responsible government by framing a new franchise.
**Electoral Expansion:** Proposed an expanded electorate, with 29,382,000 men and 6,620,000 women eligible to choose provincial legislature members, compared to the current figures of 6,792,281 men and 315,651 women.
**Federal Assembly Electorate:** Recommended a federal assembly electorate consisting of 6,864,899 men and 1,578,151 women, a significant increase from the existing combined total of 1,142,948.
**Reserved Seats for Women:** Suggested that between 2 percent and 5 percent of seats in both provincial legislatures and the assembly should be temporarily reserved for women.
**Educational and Property Qualifications:** Proposed the introduction of educational and property qualifications for voters, with special provisions to ensure the enfranchisement of women, untouchables, and labor or other minority groups.
**Exclusivity to British India:** The report dealt exclusively with British India, and the princes of the various states were left to decide on the method of election of their representatives to the federal assembly.

statementLines: Consider the following statements about Jawaharlal Nehru:

2. He presided over the Constituent Assembly.

Of these statements:
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements about Jawaharlal Nehru: 1. He was the president of the Congress Party in 1947. 2. He presided over the Constituent Assembly. 3. He formed the first Congress Ministry in United Province before India's Independence. Of these statements:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is incorrect:**Acharya J.B Kriplani was the President of Indian National Congress at the time of partition**of India in**1947**. He also served in the interim government of India (**1946**-**1947**) and the Constituent Assembly of India.**
**Statement 2 is incorrect:**Dr Sachchidananda Sinha**, the oldest member, was elected as the temporary President of the Assembly, following the French practice.** Later, on December 11, **1946**, Dr Rajendra Prasad and H C Mukherjee were elected as the President and Vice-President of the Assembly respectively.
**Statement 3 is incorrect: The first Congress Ministry in United Province was formed in july**1937**and was headed by**Pandit Govind Ballabh Pant**.**

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which one of the following was an emigre Communist journal of M.N. Roy?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following was an emigre Communist journal of M.N. Roy?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Vanguard, an emigre Communist journal, was established by M.N. Roy.** In the early 1920s, it served as a platform for disseminating communist ideology and revolutionary ideas.
This influential journal played a pivotal role in shaping the early Indian communist movement and was widely read among intellectuals and revolutionaries.
