statementLines: Mahatma Gandhi set up an Ashram on the banks of Sabarmati near Ahmedabad is known as:
questions.questionText: Mahatma Gandhi set up an Ashram on the banks of Sabarmati near Ahmedabad is known as:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The **Satyagraha Ashram** was founded on May 25, **1915** in Ahmedabad at Kochrab, when Gandhi returned from South Africa, with 25 inmates.
The Ashram was shifted on the bank of river Sabarmati on 17 June, **1917**. It was one of the main centres of the Indian freedom struggle and Gandhi's residence until **1930** It was from here that Gandhi led the famous Dandi March or the Salt Satyagraha in **1930**.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: The British Monarch at the time of Indian Independence was-
questions.questionText: The British Monarch at the time of Indian Independence was-
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **George VI** was the British monarch at the time of India's independence in **1947**. He succeeded his brother, Edward VIII, who abdicated the throne in **1936**. He played a significant role in the transition of power from Britain to India.
He appointed Lord Mountbatten on the advice of Britain Prime Minister Clement Atlee. He served as the King of the United Kingdom and the Dominions of the British Commonwealth from December 11, **1936**, until his death.
He held the position of the last Emperor of India and was also the first Head of the Commonwealth.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Hill Assembly Plan' was set up for the development of Adivasis by-
questions.questionText: Hill Assembly Plan' was set up for the development of Adivasis by-
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Cleveland** was the district collector of Bhagalpur in Bihar and he introduced the **'Hill Assembly Plan'** for the development of Adivasis in 1784. He placed the entire range of hills inhabited by Pahariyas under a uniform administration of Hill assembly under hereditary hill chiefs, called sardars.
The assembly met twice a year and had wide powers including that of inflicting or rescinding capital punishment. Sardars were entrusted the duty of reporting all crimes and law and order problems in the villages under their control to the authorities.
Cleveland also established a regular market for Adivasis to sell forest produces and provided arms to Paharia archers like regular police. He was regarded as the 'Father of the Pahariyas' by them and his death in 1784 was mourned by them.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: The export of slaves from Bengal was banned in which year?
questions.questionText: The export of slaves from Bengal was banned in which year?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. Governor-General of Bengal declared a proclamation on July 22, **1789**, banning the export of slaves from the Bengal Presidency.
Slavery was a prevalent social institution in Bengal in the 18th century, and many slaves were exported to other regions by Dutch, Burmese, and Portuguese traders and pirates.
The proclamation of 1789 was one of the first attempts to curb the slave trade in India. Slavery was finally abolished in British India by the Indian Slavery Act of 1843.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: Public Works Department was organized in 1845-1855 by-
questions.questionText: Public Works Department was organized in 1845-1855 by-
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Lord Dalhousie** was the Governor-General of India from 1848 to 1856, and he founded the **Public Works Department (PWD)** as a separate branch of the Government of India in 1854. He established a central agency for execution of public works and set up Ajmer Provincial Division.
He also appointed a committee under Sir Macdonald Stephenson to survey and plan the railway routes in India. The PWD was responsible for the construction and maintenance of roads, railways, bridges, irrigation, and other public utility works in India.
Lord Dalhousie's initiative was aimed at improving the infrastructure and development of the country, as well as facilitating trade, commerce, communication, and military operations.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who was the first Indian selected as a member of the British Parliament?
questions.questionText: Who was the first Indian selected as a member of the British Parliament?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Dadabhai Naoroji** was the first Indian selected as a member of the British Parliament. He was elected as the Member of Parliament (MP) for Finsbury Central as a candidate of the Liberal Party in the 1892 general election.
Notably, he refused to take the oath on the Bible due to his Zoroastrian faith and instead took the oath on his copy of the Khordeh Avesta. During his time in Parliament, he worked to improve the situation in India and advocated for Indian issues, making him a significant figure in the Indian independence movement.
**

Important Tips Other Indian origin leaders who were members of British Parliament: **Mancherjee Merwanjee Bhownaggree KCIE (1851 -**1933**)** was a British Conservative Party politician with Indian Parsi ancestry.
He served as a Member of Parliament (MP) representing the Bethnal Green Northeast constituency in the United Kingdom's Parliament (1895
- **1906**).
Notably, he was the third British MP of Indian descent, following in the footsteps of David Ochterlony Dyce Sombre and his fellow Parsi compatriot Dadabhai Naoroji.
**David Ochterlony Dyce Sombre (**1808-18**51)**, also known as D. O. Dyce Sombre and David Dyce Sombre, was an Anglo-Indian held to be the first person of Asian descent to be elected to the British Parliament.
He was elected to represent the Sudbury constituency in July 1841, but was removed in April 1842 due to bribery in the election.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: January 26,1950 was chosen for the enforcement of the Constitution because:
questions.questionText: January 26,1950 was chosen for the enforcement of the Constitution because:

options.b: 'Quit India Movement was begun on this date in 1942.
options.c: Congress had celebrated 26 January 1930 as the first Independence Day.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. On January 26, **1930**, during the Lahore session of the Congress under the leadership of Jawaharlal Nehru, it was declared as the **"Purna Swaraj day" or first/complete independence day**.
So, to align with that significant day, the new constitution was enacted on January 26, **1950**, marking the 20th anniversary of the Purna Swaraj day.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who had proposed "Party Less Democracy' in India?
questions.questionText: Who had proposed "Party Less Democracy' in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Jayaprakash Narayan** advocated for a **"Party Less Democracy"** in India. He believed that while parties, elections, and the constitution were crucial for democracy, true democracy would only flourish when moral values and spiritual qualities were ingrained in the public.
He argued that the party system often led to a disconnect between people and their representatives, emphasizing the need for a more direct and morally grounded democratic process.
**

Important Tips Jayaprakash Narayan proposed four key suggestions for implementing a partyless democracy: **Abolish Political Parties**: Narayan recommended the elimination of political parties from the democratic system.
Instead, he advocated for direct elections of candidates at all levels, from villages to the central government. Each gram sabha would elect two representatives to the electoral council for their constituency, where candidates for state legislatures or the central parliament would be proposed and supported in open meetings, ensuring public participation.
Candidates with over 30% of the vote would become representatives. **Free Sarvodaya Society from Party Politics**: Narayan emphasized the need to separate the Sarvodaya society from party politics, allowing it to operate independently.
**Invite All Parties to Participate in Sarvodaya Work**: He suggested inviting all political parties to participate in Sarvodaya activities, fostering cooperation and ending the divisive spirit of political parties.
**Candidates to Break from Parties After Election**: Narayan proposed that elected candidates should dissociate themselves from their respective parties after winning elections.
This would allow them to represent the people independently and avoid the constraints of party ideologies and discipline.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who authored the book "Planned Economy for India"?
questions.questionText: Who authored the book "Planned Economy for India"?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **M. Visvesvaraya** was a noted Indian engineer, scholar, statesman, and the Diwan of Mysore from **1912** to **1918**. He was a recipient of the Indian Republic's highest honor, the Bharat Ratna, in **1955**. Visvesvaraya was also the first to write about the economic planning of the country in a book titled **'Planned Economy for India'** published in **1934**. The book proposed a ten-year plan with the aim of doubling the income of the country.
His main emphasis was on industrialization so as to reduce the population dependent on agriculture and to increase the population employed in the industry.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who was the last Governor-General of Independent India?
questions.questionText: Who was the last Governor-General of Independent India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Popularly known as Rajaji, **Chakravarti Rajagopalachari** was the **first and last Indian Governor-General of Independent India** from **1948** to **1950** and he was also first Indian Governor of Madras from **1952** to **1954**. He was the supporter of Gandhi and participated in Non-Cooperation Movement in **1920**. He also edited Gandhi's newspaper 'Young India'.
He also authored book "Environmentalism: A Global History".

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which of the following assumed sovereign power at midnight of 14/15 August, 1947 provisionally?
questions.questionText: Which of the following assumed sovereign power at midnight of 14/15 August, 1947 provisionally?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The **Constituent Assembly** provisionally assumed sovereign power at midnight on August 14/15, **1947**. In a special session held in New Delhi, where Jawaharlal Nehru, India's first Prime Minister, delivered a historic speech at midnight on August 14, **1947**, in the Constituent Assembly.
His speech was brimming with inspiring ideas as the nation embarked on a new journey to draft a Constitution, leading to India becoming a Sovereign, Socialist, Secular, Democratic, and Republic nation.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which part of India remained under Portuguese control after 15th August 1947?
questions.questionText: Which part of India remained under Portuguese control after 15th August 1947?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. After August 15, **1947**, the part of India that remained under Portuguese control was **Goa**.
Goa was a Portuguese colony and continued to be under Portuguese rule until it was annexed by India in December **1961**. Portugal recognized Indian control over Goa in a treaty signed on December 31, **1974**, following the Carnation Revolution and the fall of the Estado Novo regime in Portugal.
**

Important Tips Timeline of Goa's Integration into the Indian Union: **Post-Independence Relations (**1947**-**1949**)**: After India gained independence in **1947**, diplomatic relations were established with Portugal in **1949**.
**Deterioration of Relations (**1950**)**: Relations between India and Portugal deteriorated due to Portugal's refusal to surrender its enclaves on India's west coast, including Goa, Daman and Diu, and Dadra and Nagar Haveli.
**Recognition as Overseas Province (**1951**)**: In **1951**, Portugal changed its constitution, recognizing Goa as an overseas province rather than a colonial possession.
This move was seen as an attempt to invoke NATO's collective security clause in case of an Indian attack. **Diplomatic Break (**1955**)**: By **1955**, diplomatic relations between India and Portugal had been severed, leading to a crisis.
**Liberation of Goa (**1961**)**: In **1961**, India launched Operation Vijay on December 19th, leading to the annexation of Daman and Diu, as well as Goa, and the end of Portuguese rule over these enclaves.
Operation Vijay involved air, sea and land strikes for over 36 hours, and was a decisive victory for India, ending 451 years of rule by Portugal over its remaining exclaves in India.
The engagement lasted two days, and twenty-two Indians and thirty Portuguese were killed in the fighting.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: Which of the following Governor-General used the system of Separate electoral college to conquer Muslims and make them against Congress?
questions.questionText: Which of the following Governor-General used the system of Separate electoral college to conquer Muslims and make them against Congress?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Lord Minto** was the Governor-General of India from **1905** to **1910**, and he used the system of separate electorates to win the support of the Muslims and to create a rift between them and the Congress.
He implemented the **Morley-Minto Reforms in 1909**, which granted separate electorates to Muslims for the election of members of provincial and imperial legislative councils.
This meant that Muslims could vote only for Muslim candidates, and not for candidates from other communities. The reforms also increased the number of elected Indian members in the councils, but gave them limited powers and functions.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Match the following female Rulers with their State/Capital:
1. A. Rani Durgavati -
1. Jhansi
2. B. Maharani Ahilya Bai -
2. Holkar State 3.
C. Maharani Lakshmi Bai -
3. Gara Mandal
4. D. Begum Razia Sultan -
4. Delhi Code:
questions.questionText: Match the following female Rulers with their State/Capital: 1. A. Rani Durgavati - 1. Jhansi 2. B. Maharani Ahilya Bai - 2. Holkar State 3. C. Maharani Lakshmi Bai - 3. Gara Mandal 4. D. Begum Razia Sultan - 4. Delhi Code:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.
- **Rani Durgavati** was the ruling Queen of Garha Kingdom, also called **Garha Mandla** or Garha Katanga from 1550 until 1564. She was married to Dalpat Shah, the son of king Sangram Shah of the Garha Kingdom. She is chiefly remembered for defending her kingdom against the **Mughal** Empire.
- **Rani Lakshmibai** of **Jhansi**, a prominent figure in the Indian Rebellion of 1857, valiantly resisted British rule. After her husband's death and the British annexation of Jhansi, she led the rebellion, defending Jhansi successfully but ultimately falling in battle at Gwalior in 1858.
- **Razia Sultana**, the daughter of Iltutmish, made history as the first female Muslim ruler of **Delhi**. Her ascension faced challenges from nobles, leading to her deposition in 1240 after a brief rule of less than four years. She made a failed attempt to regain power and was ultimately killed.
- **Ahilya Bai Holkar** was the hereditary noble queen of the Maratha Empire, in early-modern India. She established Maheshwar (in Madhya Pradesh) as the seat of **Holkar Dynasty**. She defended Malwa from invaders, led armies alongside her military commander Tukoji Rao Holkar, and is renowned for constructing numerous Hindu temples and Dharmashalas across India.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: The author of the novel 'Chandrakanta' is:
questions.questionText: The author of the novel 'Chandrakanta' is:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **"Chandrakanta"** is an epic fantasy Hindi novel written by **Devaki Nandan Khatri** in 1888. It is the first modern Hindi novel and played a significant role in popularizing the Hindi language.
The story revolves around the love between Princess Chandrakanta of Vijaygarh and Prince Virendra Singh of Naugarh, set against the backdrop of rival kingdoms and magical tilisms (enchantments).
The novel has inspired adaptations, including a popular TV series, and has several sequels exploring further adventures in magical realms.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: Which Governor- General was prosecuted for impeachment?
questions.questionText: Which Governor- General was prosecuted for impeachment?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Warren Hastings** was the first governor-general of Bengal from 1772 to 1785, and he was **impeached by the British Parliament in 1787** on charges of misconduct and corruption during his time in Calcutta.
The trial lasted for seven years and involved 22 charges against Hastings, such as the Rohilla War, the case of Nanda Kumar, the treatment of Raja Chait Singh of Benares, and the pressures on the Begums of Oudh.
Hastings defended himself vigorously and was eventually acquitted in 1795, but his reputation and fortune were damaged by the ordeal.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: What idea is given by Gandhiji for family planning?
questions.questionText: What idea is given by Gandhiji for family planning?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The idea given by Gandhiji for family planning was **self-control**. He believed that self-control was the best and the most natural way of regulating the size of the family and preventing unwanted pregnancies.
He said, "Self-control is the surest and the only method of regulating the birth-rate. Birth-control by contraceptives is race-suicide." He also said, "The use of contraceptives is likely to produce evils of which we have no conception. Gandhiji was opposed to any artificial or mechanical methods of family planning, such as sterilization, restrain, or loop. He considered them as immoral, unnatural, and harmful to the health and dignity of the individuals and the society.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who wrote the book 'Prison Diary'?
questions.questionText: Who wrote the book 'Prison Diary'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The book **"Prison Diary"** was written by **Jai Prakash Narayan (JP)**. It is a collection of JP's writings and reflections during his imprisonment during the Emergency period in India, which lasted from June **1975** to March **1977**. In the book, JP shares his thoughts, experiences, and observations about the political environment of the time, as well as his personal struggles and loneliness while in prison.
The diary provides valuable insights into the challenges faced by political prisoners during that period.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Name the revolutionary woman, who participated in the Peasant Movement of Bijoliya and was arrested. She also participated in the 1930 Satyragrah and 1932 Civil Disobedience Movement and was sentenced to jail:
questions.questionText: Name the revolutionary woman, who participated in the Peasant Movement of Bijoliya and was arrested. She also participated in the 1930 Satyragrah and 1932 Civil Disobedience Movement and was sentenced to jail:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Ramadevi Choudhury**, also known as **Rama Devi**, was a revolutionary woman who played a significant role in India's independence movement.
She actively participated in various movements, including the Peasant Movement of Bijoliya, the **1930** Satyagraha, and the **1932** Civil Disobedience Movement.
Due to her dedication to the cause, she was arrested multiple times and sentenced to jail. Rama Devi was highly influenced by Mahatma Gandhi and worked tirelessly to mobilize women and promote the ideals of the independence struggle.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: Who among the following negotiated Subordinate Alliances of 1817-18 with the Princely States of Rajputana?
questions.questionText: Who among the following negotiated Subordinate Alliances of 1817-18 with the Princely States of Rajputana?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Charles Metcalf** was the British Resident at Delhi who negotiated **Subordinate Alliances of**1817-18 with the princely states of Rajputana.
These alliances brought the Rajputana states under the complete subordination of the British. **

Important Tips **Charles Metcalf** was a British colonial administrator who served as the Governor-General of India from 1835 to 1836.
He also played a key role in suppressing the Pindari raids and the Third Anglo-Maratha War. He was known for his liberal and reformist policies, such as abolishing censorship of the press and granting equal rights to Indians.
He was also a scholar of Indian languages and culture, and wrote several books and essays on Indian history and society.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: The revolutionary, who was not involved in Hardinge Bomb incident?
questions.questionText: The revolutionary, who was not involved in Hardinge Bomb incident?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Bhagwati Charan Vohra was not involved in the Hardinge Bomb incident.** He was an Indian revolutionary, associated with Hindustan Socialist Republican Association.
He along with Bhagat Singh and Sukhdev studied on the model of the Russian Socialist Revolution. In **1929**, he planned bomb blast under the train of Viceroy Lord Irwin on the Delhi-Agra railway line.
The viceroy escaped unhurt. Mahatma Gandhi condemned the revolutionary act through his article The Cult of Bomb. **

Important Tips **Delhi Conspiracy Case**:
The Delhi-Lahore conspiracy is known for the bombing of incident Viceroy of India Lord Hardinge. The event took place when he was travelling to Delhi on the occasion of the relocation of the capital from Calcutta to New Delhi.
**Rasbihari Bose** is considered the pioneer of this conspiracy. Other important revolutionaries who were involved in this act were: **Basant Kumar Vishwas**, **Bal Mukund**, **Awadh Bihari** and **Master Amir Chand** They were hanged on the charges of this crime.
Rasbihari Bose escaped arrest and escaped to Japan.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who has authored 'Gandhian Constitution for Free India'?
questions.questionText: Who has authored 'Gandhian Constitution for Free India'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The book **"Gandhian Constitution for Free India"** was authored by **Shriman Narayan Agarwal**.
Published in **1946**, it focuses on Mahatma Gandhi's ideals of non-violence, harmony, and his concepts on education, trade, and property. Agarwal's work seeks to synthesize these Gandhian principles into a document that could inspire the direction taken by independent India.
The book analyzes Gandhian philosophy to outline insights into framing a constitution for a free India, emphasizing the importance of combining fundamental rights with the duties of the people.
Mahatma Gandhi himself found the ideas in the book consistent with his own beliefs.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who was the first historian who wrote about 'Feudalism' in Rajasthan during the 19th Century?
questions.questionText: Who was the first historian who wrote about 'Feudalism' in Rajasthan during the 19th Century?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The first historian to write about 'Feudalism' in Rajasthan during the 19th century was **Col. James Tod**.
He documented the contemporary geography and history of Rajputana and Central India, along with the history of the Rajput clans ruling the region at that time, in his work **"Annals and Antiquities of Rajasthan."** His writing, published in two volumes in 1829 and 1832, included illustrations and engravings by notable artists and significantly influenced the popular and academic understanding of Rajasthan's history and heroes.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: The main aim of East India Company to make a subsidiary alliance in Rajput states was-
questions.questionText: The main aim of East India Company to make a subsidiary alliance in Rajput states was-

options.b: Protecting these states from Maratha-Pindari invasion
options.c: To receive funds in the form of Khiraj
options.d: To establish the sovereignty of the British

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The main aim of the East India Company to make a subsidiary alliance in Rajput states was to **extend its political and economic control over them and to prevent any possible challenge from them or other powers**.
The subsidiary alliance was a system devised by Lord Wellesley, the Governor-General of India from 1798 to 1805, by which the Indian rulers had to accept the presence and maintenance of a British force in their territory and surrender their foreign policy to the British.
In return, the British promised to protect them from any external or internal threats. **

Important Tips The Rajput states were among the last to enter into the subsidiary alliance with the British, as they had resisted their domination for a long time.
They were also reluctant to give up their autonomy and sovereignty to the foreign power. However, they were eventually forced or persuaded to sign the treaty after the Third Anglo-Maratha War (**1817-18**18), which resulted in the defeat of the Maratha Confederacy and the annexation of most of its territories by the British.
The Rajput states that signed the subsidiary alliance with the British were: Jaipur (1818), Jodhpur (1818), Udaipur (1818), Kota (1818), Bundi (1818), Bikaner (1818), Alwar (1818), Bharatpur (1826).

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Which one of the following Jails was named as 'Mandir' by Gandhiji?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following Jails was named as 'Mandir' by Gandhiji?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Yarvada Central Jail** is the largest jail in the state of Maharashtra. Mahatma Gandhi spent several years during India's struggle for freedom. He named Yarvada Jail as **Mandir**. He wrote 'Yerwada Mandir' (Ashrama Observance) first published in **1932**, translated from Gujarati, by Valji Govind Desai.
**

Important Tips **Naini**: Naini Central Prison is a jail located in Allahabad, Uttar Pradesh. It was built by the British in 1860 and was used to imprison many freedom fighters and revolutionaries such as Chandra Shekhar Azad, Ram Prasad Bismil, Ashfaqulla Khan, and Roshan Singh.
**Cellular (Port Blair)**: Cellular Jail is a colonial prison located in Port Blair, Andaman and Nicobar Islands. It was built by the British between 1896 and **1906** and was used to exile political prisoners and activists who opposed the British rule in India.
It was also known as "Kala Pani" or "Black Water" because of the harsh conditions and torture faced by the inmates. **Aghakhan Palace**: Aghakhan Palace is a historical building located in Pune, Maharashtra.
It was built by Sultan Muhammed Shah Aga Khan III in 1892 and was donated to India by his son Aga Khan IV in **1969**. It was used as a prison by the British during the Quit India Movement in **1942** and housed Mahatma Gandhi, Kasturba Gandhi, Mahadev Desai, Sarojini Naidu, and others.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: What does 'Gandhian Innovation' mean?
questions.questionText: What does 'Gandhian Innovation' mean?

options.d: To produce more from less input for more people

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Gandhian Innovation** means **to produce more from less input for more people**. It is a term coined by Prof. C.K. Prahalad and Dr. R.A. Mashelkar to describe a type of innovation that aims to serve the needs of the poor and the marginalized by creating affordable and sustainable products and services with fewer resources.
One of the examples of Gandhian Innovation is the Jaipur Foot, which is a prosthetic limb that can be fitted in less than an hour and costs only $30, compared to $8,000 for a similar product in the West.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: The writer of 'Indian National Movement: The Long-Term Dynamics' is:
questions.questionText: The writer of 'Indian National Movement: The Long-Term Dynamics' is:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The book **"Indian National Movement: The Long-Term Dynamics"** is written by **Bipan Chandra**.
This book is a significant contribution to the study of India's freedom struggle and explores various aspects of the movement, including its ideology, program, the role of non-violence, the relationship between leaders and the masses, and the movement's capacity for ideological transformation.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: According to Gandhiji, the Cruelest form of violence is-
questions.questionText: According to Gandhiji, the Cruelest form of violence is-
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Gandhi said that **poverty is the worst form of violence** because it deprives people of their basic human needs and dignity.
He said, "Poverty is the cruelest form of violence. It strikes not only at the individual, but at his family and his future generations as well." He also said, "There is enough for everyone's need, but not for everyone's greed." Gandhi was deeply concerned about the plight of the poor and the oppressed in India and elsewhere.
He fought against the British colonial rule and the exploitation of the Indian masses by the landlords and the capitalists. He also campaigned for social reforms such as abolition of untouchability, emancipation of women, and promotion of education and health.
He advocated for a simple and self-reliant lifestyle based on the principles of truth, non-violence, and service. He said, "The best way to find yourself is to lose yourself in the service of others."

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Where did Gandhiji adopt 'Seva Dharma'?
questions.questionText: Where did Gandhiji adopt 'Seva Dharma'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Mahatma Gandhi adopted the concept of 'Seva Dharma' or the duty of service during his stay in South Africa, where he rendered his services as an attendant to the helpless poor patients in the charity hospitals of Rustumji.
He also served there the wounded soldiers of the Boer War. He said, "The best way to find yourself is to lose yourself in the service of others." 'Seva Dharma' was one of the key principles of Gandhi's philosophy and practice.
He believed that service to humanity is service to God, and that everyone should dedicate their lives to the welfare of others. He said, "Service without humility is selfishness and egotism." He also said, "Service which is rendered without joy helps neither the servant nor the served. But all other pleasures and possessions pale into nothingness before service which is rendered in a spirit of joy."

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Which one of the following was the last step in the Gandhian strategy of Satyagraha?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following was the last step in the Gandhian strategy of Satyagraha?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The strike was the last step in the Gandhian strategy of satyagraha, while fasting was the first step.
Gandhi termed fasting as 'Fire Arrow'. He used strike as a means of exerting pressure on the authorities and demonstrating the strength and unity of the people.
He also used boycott, picket, and civil disobedience as other methods of satyagraha in India. **

Important Tips Satyagraha was a nonviolent resistance to evil based on the principles of truth and moral courage.
Candhi coined the term in **1908** to distinguish it from passive resistance, which he felt was inadequate and misleading. He derived the concept from the ancient Indian ideal of ahimsa (non-injury), as well as from the writings of Leo Tolstoy, Henry David Thoreau, the Bible, and the Bhagavad Gita.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Which of the following statements is not true as per Gandhian Principle?
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements is not true as per Gandhian Principle?

options.a: The aim of Satyagrahi is to defeat the enemy
options.b: The weapon of Satyagraha is Ahimsa
options.c: Satyagrahi should be firm in his belief
options.d: Satyagrahi should have no ill feeling towards his enemies

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The statement that is not true as per Gandhian Principle is-The aim of Satyagrahi is to defeat the enemy.
This is because Gandhi did not consider his opponents as enemies, but as friends who were misguided or ignorant of the truth. He said, "I do not believe in short-victories over an opponent. I do not want to end his wickedness by destroying him. I want to convert him." He also said, "The real victory is when your opponent realizes the truth and changes his mind." Therefore, the aim of Satyagrahi was not to defeat the enemy, but to win him over by appealing to his conscience and reason.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: As per Mahatma Gandhi politics meant-
questions.questionText: As per Mahatma Gandhi politics meant-
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. Mahatma Gandhi believed that politics meant the service of the people and the nation. He said, "Politics divorced from religion has absolutely no meaning for me." He also said, "Politics should be sacred and not a game of parties. Gandhi's politics was based on the principles of truth, non-violence, and self-rule. He fought for India's independence from the British colonial rule and for the upliftment of the oppressed and exploited sections of the society. He also advocated for social reforms such as abolition of untouchability, emancipation of women, promotion of education and health, and protection of environment.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Which of the following is not a feature of politics of the Gandhian model?
questions.questionText: Which of the following is not a feature of politics of the Gandhian model?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. Gandhi did not believe in the legitimacy or necessity of any form of centralized authority or state.
He said, "I look upon an increase in the power of the State with the greatest fear because, although while apparently doing good by minimizing exploitation, it does the greatest harm to mankind by destroying individuality which lies at the root of all progress." He also said, "The State represents violence in a concentrated and organized form. The individual has a soul, but as the State is a soulless machine, it can never be weaned from violence to which it owes its very existence."

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Gandhi's boycott of British made products was effective because the British considered India, a major:
questions.questionText: Gandhi's boycott of British made products was effective because the British considered India, a major:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Mahatma Gandhi's boycott of British made products was effective because the British considered India as a major market for their goods.
They exported various items such as textiles, machinery, iron, steel, and salt to India and made huge profits from them. They also imposed high tariffs and taxes on Indian goods and discouraged the development of indigenous industries.
This resulted in the exploitation and impoverishment of the Indian people. Gandhi launched the swadeshi or self-reliance movement in **1905**, which involved boycotting foreign goods and promoting Indian goods.
He encouraged people to burn or discard their foreign clothes and wear khadi or hand-spun cloth instead. He also supported the growth of cottage industries, such as spinning, weaving, pottery, and leatherwork. He believed that this would not only weaken the economic and political hold of the British over India, but also foster a sense of national pride and dignity among the Indians.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Whose work has influenced the Gandhian concept?
questions.questionText: Whose work has influenced the Gandhian concept?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. Mahatma Gandhi's concept was influenced by the works of Ruskin, Thoreau, and Tolstoy, who were all secular writers and thinkers.
They shared some common themes and values, such as simplicity, truth, non-violence, and social justice. Gandhi read their books and essays and applied their ideas to his own philosophy and practice. **

Important Tips Some of the works that influenced Gandhi's concept are:
Unto This Last by John Ruskin: This was a book that Gandhi read in **1904**, when he was in South Africa. It was a critique of the capitalist system and a plea for a moral economy based on the dignity of labour and the well-being of all.
Civil Disobedience by Henry David Thoreau: This was an essay that Gandhi read in **1907**, when he was facing a legal crisis in South Africa.
It was a defense of the right and duty of the individual to resist unjust laws and authority. The Kingdom of God is Within You by Leo Tolstoy: This was a book that Gandhi read in **1908**, when he was in prison in South Africa.
It was a religious and philosophical treatise that advocated for a life of love, peace, and non-violence based on the teachings of Jesus Christ.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Which of the following statements is/are correct regarding Gandhi?
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements is/are correct regarding Gandhi?

options.a: Fought hard to improve the status of casteless untouchables

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. All the statements are correct regarding Gandhi and his political life. He fought hard to improve the status of casteless untouchables, whom he called Harijans or the children of God.
He launched the Non-Cooperation Movement in **1920**, which involved boycotting foreign goods, institutions, titles, and honours, and promoting swadeshi, education, and self-governance.
He began the Civil Disobedience Movement in **1930** with the famous Dandi March or Salt Satyagraha, in which he defied the British salt monopoly by making salt from seawater.
He also led other movements and campaigns based on the principles of truth, non-violence, and self-rule.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Which one of the following statements is not correct about Gandhian economy?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements is not correct about Gandhian economy?

options.a: He laid emphasis on the economy based on non-violence.
options.b: Centralization led to exploitation and is inequality; hence centralization is opponent of formation of non-violent society
options.c: He was not in favour of mechanization in India
options.d: He did not favour mechanization in U.S.A.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. Gandhi was not opposed to mechanization in general, but only to the extent that it harmed human dignity, morality and well-being.
He believed that machines should be used as a means to serve humanity, not to exploit or enslave it. He also recognized that different countries had different levels of development and needs, and that what was suitable for India might not be suitable for other nations.
Therefore, Gandhi did not object to mechanization in U.S.A. or other advanced countries, as long as it did not violate the principles of non-violence, truth and justice.
He wrote: "I do not want my house to be walled in on all sides and my windows to be stuffed. I want the cultures of all lands to be blown about my house as freely as possible. But I refuse to be blown off my feet by any."

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: According to M.K. Gandhi socio-economic improvement of untouchables can be brought about:
questions.questionText: According to M.K. Gandhi socio-economic improvement of untouchables can be brought about:

options.c: By earmarking funds for their socio-economic development
options.d: By establishing cottage industry for them

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. According to M.K. Gandhi, socio-economic improvement of untouchables can be brought about by establishing cottage industry for them.
He believed that cottage industry, especially spinning and weaving, would provide employment, income and dignity to the untouchables, who were often denied access to other occupations by the caste system.
He also thought that cottage industry would promote self-reliance, simplicity and swadeshi among the untouchables and the rest of the society.
He considered untouchability as a sin and a blot on Hinduism, and fought against it throughout his life.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Consider the following statements:
1. The Arya Samaj was founded in 1875.
2. 'Al Hilal' was published by Maulana Abul Kalam Azad.
3. Lala Lajpat Rai was not associated with the paper 'The People.
4. The famous Presidency College (former Hindu College) of Calcutta was established by Raja Ram Mohan Roy.
Of these statements:
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: 1. The Arya Samaj was founded in 1875. 2. 'Al Hilal' was published by Maulana Abul Kalam Azad. 3. Lala Lajpat Rai was not associated with the paper 'The People. 4. The famous Presidency College (former Hindu College) of Calcutta was established by Raja Ram Mohan Roy. Of these statements:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is correct: Arya Samaj is considered a monotheistic Indian Hindu reform movement that promotes values and practices based on the belief in the infallible authority of the Vedas.** The samaj was founded by Dayanand Saraswati in 1875.
**Statement 2 is correct: The Al-Hilal was an Urdu language newspaper founded by Maulana Abul Kalam Azad.** The newspaper's first edition was published in Kolkata in **1912**. It was used as a tool for opposing the British Raj in India.
The newspaper also supported the Indian freedom movement's cause and encouraged Indian Muslims to join the movement.

**Statement 3 is incorrect: Along with founding 'Arya Gazette' as its editor, Lala Lajpat Rai regularly contributed to several major Hindi, Punjabi, English and Urdu newspapers and magazines.** He took out a Weekly newspaper "People" from Lahore. He himself was the editor of this paper.
**Statement 4 is correct: Hindu College was established on January 20, 1817, in Calcutta.** Raja Ram Mohan Roy, the great social reformer, headed its foundation committee.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: Consider the following statements:
1. Asaf Ali looked after the work of Railway Ministry in the interim Government (1946)
2. Ancient Monuments Preservation Act' was passed when Lord Curzon was Governor-General.
3. The Haripura Session of the Indian National Congress was presided over by C.R. Das.
4. Swami Shraddhanand suggested a no-tax campaign as a protest against the Rowlatt Act.
Of these statements:
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: 1. Asaf Ali looked after the work of Railway Ministry in the interim Government (1946) 2. Ancient Monuments Preservation Act' was passed when Lord Curzon was Governor-General. 3. The Haripura Session of the Indian National Congress was presided over by C.R. Das. 4. Swami Shraddhanand suggested a no-tax campaign as a protest against the Rowlatt Act. Of these statements:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is correct: Asaf Ali was an Indian independence activist and noted lawyer.** He looked after the work of Railway Ministry in the interim Government (**1946**).
**Statement 2 is correct: The Ancient Monuments Preservation Act,**1904**, enacted during Lord Curzon's British India tenure, aimed to safeguard historic monuments, regulate antiquities traffic and excavations, and protect and acquire valuable archaeological, historical, or artistic artifacts and sites through the Archaeological Survey of India**

**Statement 3 is incorrect: The Indian National Congress held its fifty-first session in Haripura in the Surat district in Gujarat.** It was held on 19th February **1938**, presided over by Subhash Chandra Bose.

**Statement 4 is correct: The British Colonial Government passed the "Rowlatt Act" which gave power to the police to arrest any person without any reason.** The purpose of the Act was to curb the growing nationalist upsurge in the country.
Mahatma Gandhi called upon the people to perform satyagraha against the act. Swami Sharaddhanand suggested no tax campaign (non-payment of Lagaan/Rent) in protest against Rowlatt Act.
**

Important Tips Haripura Congress Session, **1938**: The 51st session of the Indian National Congress took place in Haripura, Gujarat, in **1938**, under the leadership of Subhash Chandra Bose.
Bose emphasized the need to peacefully resist the colonial government's Federal Scheme, which was seen as undemocratic and favoring British capitalism.
There was a heated debate during the session about the princely states, with Congress ultimately declaring its goal of complete independence for all of India, including the princely states.
The Congress session demanded a responsible government and the protection of civil liberties within the states. Support for the release of political prisoners was also expressed.
Recognizing the growing peasant movements, Congress affirmed the right of Kisan Sabhas (farmers' organizations) to form unions but specified that activities conflicting with Congress principles would not be supported.
Bose called for increased cooperation between Congress, Trade Union Congress, and peasant organizations. The session concluded with the election of Subhash Chandra Bose as the next Congress session's president, solidifying Congress's commitment to India's independence struggle.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Who wrote the book "Gokhale- my political guru"?
questions.questionText: Who wrote the book "Gokhale- my political guru"?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. "Gokhale: My Political Guru" is a book written by Mahatma Gandhi. In this book, Gandhi reflects on his relationship with Gopal Krishna Gokhale, a prominent leader and political mentor.
He discusses the deep influence Gokhale had on him and his political philosophy. Gandhi emphasizes Gokhale's commitment to bringing a spiritual and ethical dimension to politics, which greatly impacted his own approach to leadership and social change.
**

Important Tips Popular books written by M.K.Gandhi: An Autobiography or The Story of My Experiments with Truth: This autobiography is a detailed account of Gandhi's life, his experiments with truth, spirituality, and his journey towards becoming the Mahatma.
Hind Swaraj or Indian Home Rule: In this influential work, Gandhi presents his vision of self-rule for India, emphasizing the importance of traditional Indian values and nonviolent resistance.
Truth is God: This book delves into Gandhi's beliefs focusing on truth and spirituality, rather than religious dogma. India of My Dreams: Gandhi's vision for an independent India is outlined in this collection of writings and speeches, covering various aspects of socio-political and economic life.
The Bhagavad Gita: Gandhi found inspiration in this revered Hindu scripture, which guided his principles of nonviolence and truth. The Law and The Lawyers: In this thought-provoking book, Gandhi critiques the legal system and the role of lawyers, advocating for truth and service above all.
Gokhale My Political Guru: Gandhi pays tribute to his mentor, Gopal Krishna Gokhale, sharing his experiences and learnings from this great leader.
Diet and Diet Reform: Gandhi's experiments with diet and his pursuit of a balanced and healthy lifestyle are documented in this work. Satyagraha in South Africa: This book provides insights into Gandhi's experiences with Satyagraha, his nonviolent resistance movement, during his time in South Africa.
Ashram Observances in Action: Gandhi explains the observances followed in his ashram, addressing themes like poverty, work, love, and truth, offering guidance for a purposeful life.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: M.K. Gandhi was a supporter of-
questions.questionText: M.K. Gandhi was a supporter of-
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. M.K. Gandhi was a supporter of philosophical anarchism. He believed in the principle of swaraj or self-rule, which meant not only political independence from British colonialism, but also individual and collective autonomy from any form of centralized authority.
He advocated for a decentralized and nonviolent society based on the values of truth, nonviolence, and compassion. He rejected the idea of a state or a government as a necessary evil, and instead envisioned a network of self-reliant villages and communities that would cooperate with each other without coercion or domination.
**

Important Tips Philosophical anarchism is a political philosophy that advocates for a society without any form of state or government, and instead relies on the voluntary cooperation and mutual aid of individuals and communities.
Some of the most influential philosophical anarchists in history are William Godwin, Henry David Thoreau, Leo Tolstoy, Emma Goldman, and Mahatma Gandhi.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Aurobindo Ghosh wrote:
questions.questionText: Aurobindo Ghosh wrote:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. Aurobindo Ghosh authored "The Life Divine," a comprehensive work that blends Western thought and Eastern spirituality, offering original insights.
It explores themes such as human aspiration, the emergence of life from a Divine Source, evolution from matter to spirit, human consciousness, and the spiritual destiny of life on Earth.
It was first published serially in the Arya from **1914** to **1919** and later in book form in **1939** and **1940**, with subsequent editions.
**

Important Tips Some of the well-known works by Sri Aurobindo: Savitri: A Legend and A Symbol (poetry) The Life Divine The Renaissance in India with A Defense of Indian Culture Hymns to the Mystic Fire The Secret of the Veda Essays on The Gita The Synthesis of Yoga Vedic and Philological Studies

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who made the agreement between Muslim League and Congress in 1916?
questions.questionText: Who made the agreement between Muslim League and Congress in 1916?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. B. G. Tilak made the agreement between Muslim League and Congress possible during Lucknow session, popularly called Lucknow pact of **1916**. The Lucknow Pact of **1916** is signed between Bal Gangadhar Tilak and Muhammad Ali Jinnah.
Tilak represented the Congress while framing the deal. All-India Muslim League was led by Muhammad Ali Jinnah. It was adopted by the Congress at its Lucknow session on December 29 and by the Muslim league on Dec. 31, **1916**.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who is the author of flag song (Jhanda Geet) of India?
questions.questionText: Who is the author of flag song (Jhanda Geet) of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The author of the flag song (Jhanda Geet) of India is Shyamlal Parshad **Gupta**. This patriotic song, originally composed with seven stanzas, was later modified by the Congress, recognizing three stanzas as the "Dhwajgeet" or flag song.
It was officially accepted in the Congress session of **1938**. Shyamlal **Gupta** 'Parshad' was a prominent poet and patriot known for his strong expressions of nationalism.
His song played a significant role in inspiring many to sacrifice for the country's freedom struggle.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: In whose rule the Widow Remarriage Act was implemented-
questions.questionText: In whose rule the Widow Remarriage Act was implemented-
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. . The Widow Remarriage Act was passed on 16 July 1856, during the rule of Lord Canning, who was the Governor-General of India at that time.
The act legalized the remarriage of Hindu widows in all jurisdictions of India under East India Company rule. **

Important Tips The Widow Remarriage Act was drafted by Lord Dalhousie, who was the previous Governor-General, and passed during Lord Canning before the Indian Rebellion of 1857.
The act was enacted due to the efforts of social reformer Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar, who advocated for the rights and dignity of Hindu widows.
The act provided legal safeguards against loss of certain forms of inheritance for remarrying a Hindu widow, though, under the Act, the widow forsook any inheritance due her from her deceased husband.
The act was a major step towards the empowerment of women and the abolition of harmful customs in Hindu society.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which one of the following is correctly matched?
1. A. Valentine Chirol -
1. Indian Struggle
2. B. Rafiq Zakaria -
2. The Man Who Divided India 3.
C. Subhash Chandra Bose -
3. Indian Unrest
4. D. V.D. Savarkar -
4. Unhappy India
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is correctly matched? 1. A. Valentine Chirol - 1. Indian Struggle 2. B. Rafiq Zakaria - 2. The Man Who Divided India 3. C. Subhash Chandra Bose - 3. Indian Unrest 4. D. V.D. Savarkar - 4. Unhappy India
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. Pair a is incorrectly matched: "The Indian Struggle" authored by Subhash Chandra Bose, is a two-part book that chronicles India's independence movement from **1920** to **1942**, spanning key events and movements.
Banned by the British, it was published in India in **1948** after independence. Pair b is correctly matched: Rafiq Zakaria authored the book "The Man Who Divided India," which provides a critical biography of Muhammad Ali Jinnah and explores his role in the partition of India.
This book analyzes Jinnah's leadership and its aftermath, shedding light on the factors that contributed to the division of India into India and Pakistan.
It offers insights into the complexities of the partition and its consequences Pair c is incorrectly matched: "Indian Unrest" authored by Valentine Sir Chirol, is a significant collection of articles addressing the complex issue of Indian unrest during the British Empire.
It offers valuable insights and comprehensive analysis akin to a formal investigative report on the root causes and state of affairs leading to unrest in India.
Pair d is incorrectly matched: "Unhappy India" was authored by Lala Lajpat Rai. This book was a response to Katherine Mayo's "Mother India" and aimed to counter her arguments about India and its people, highlighting the socio-economic problems caused by British colonial rule.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Which of the following Movement is not related to Gandhiji?
questions.questionText: Which of the following Movement is not related to Gandhiji?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Mahatma Gandhi was not related to the Swadeshi Movement, which was started to protest against the partition of Bengal in **1905** and continued up to **1911**. During this period, Gandhi was residing in South Africannd fighting against racial discrimination and oppression there.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: In which language the famous work of Raja Rammohan Roy 'A Gift to Monotheists' was written?
questions.questionText: In which language the famous work of Raja Rammohan Roy 'A Gift to Monotheists' was written?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Raja Ramniohan Roy's famous work "A Gift to Monotheists" (Tuhfat-ul-Muwahhidin) was written in the Persian language, with an introduction in Arabic.
This work is notable as it marks one of his early intellectual endeavors and is recognized as the first known theological statement by him before he gained fame and notoriety as a Vedantin.
During that time, Bengali had not yet become the language of intellectual discourse, and Raja Rammohan Roy's writings were primarily in Persian and Arabic.
The work was later translated into English by Maulavi Obaidullah EI Obaid and published by the Adi Brahmo Samaj in 1884. **

Important Tips Raja Ram Mohan Roy Literary Work Tuhfat-ul-Muwahhidin (1804) Vedanta Gantha (1815) Kenopanishads, Translation of an abridgment of the Vedanta Sara, Ishopanishad (1816) The Precepts of Jesus-The Guide to Peace and Happiness, A Defence of Hindu Theism (1820) Bengali Grammar (1826) History of Indian Philosophy, The Universal Religion (1829) Gaudiya Vyakaran (1833)

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: In 1929 at which place Mahatma Gandhi completed his translation of Anasakti Yoga?
questions.questionText: In 1929 at which place Mahatma Gandhi completed his translation of Anasakti Yoga?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Mahatma Gandhi completed his translation of "Anasakti Yoga" in Kausani in **1929**. This translation is a part of his commentary on the Bhagavad Gita, where he emphasized the importance of renunciation of the fruits of action and selfless devotion as central teachings for attaining self-realization and ultimate peace.
His interpretation underscores the significance of these principles in everyday life and worldly pursuits.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: Who introduced 'Financial Decentralization' in India?
questions.questionText: Who introduced 'Financial Decentralization' in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. Lord Mayo's Resolution of 1870 on Financial Decentralization visualized the development of local self-government institutions.
Its resolution pertained to financial decentralization which was a legislative devolution inaugurated by the Indian Council Act of 1861. Apart from the annual grant from the imperial government, the provincial governments were authorized to resort to local taxation to balance their budgets.
**

Important Tips Lord Mayo served as the 4th Viceroy of India from 12 January 1869 to 8 February 1872. He followed the policy of non-intervention.
India's census began during his tenure. He was assassinated at the penal settlement of Port Blair in the Andaman Islands.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: James Andrew Ramsay was the real name of which Governor-General of India?
questions.questionText: James Andrew Ramsay was the real name of which Governor-General of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. James Andrew Ramsay was the real name of the 1st Marquess of Dalhousie, who served as the Governor-General of India from 1848 to 1856. He was also known as the Earl of Dalhousie between 1838 and 1849. He was a Scottish statesman and colonial administrator who is credited with creating the map of modern India through his conquests and annexations of independent provinces.
He also introduced many reforms in India, such as railways, telegraph, postal service, public works, education, and administration. He is also infamous for his policy of the Doctrine of Lapse, which allowed the British to annex any Indian state whose ruler died without a natural heir.
This policy was widely resented by the Indian princes and contributed to the outbreak of the Indian Rebellion of 1857, one year after his retirement.
**

Important Tips The Doctrine of Lapse was a policy of annexation that was applied by the British East India Company in India until 1858. It stated that any princely state under the control of the Company would be annexed if the ruler died without a male heir or was incompetent.
The policy was devised by the Court of Directors of the Company in 1834, but it was widely used by Lord Dalhousie, who was the Governor-General of India from 1848 to 1856.
He annexed several states under this policy, such as Satara, Jhansi, Nagpur, and Tanjore. The policy was seen as illegitimate and disrespectful by many Indians, and it was one of the causes of the Indian Rebellion of 1857.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Which one of the following Ashrams related to Mahatma Gandhi, is the oldest?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following Ashrams related to Mahatma Gandhi, is the oldest?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The oldest ashram related to Mahatma Gandhi is the Phoenix Ashram in South Africa. He established this ashram in **1904**, after being inspired by the writings of Leo Tolstoy and John Ruskin. He named the ashram after a magazine called The Phoenix, which he published from there.
The ashram was a place where Gandhi and his followers practiced the principles of truth, non-violence, and self-reliance. **

Important Tips Wardha Ashram: This ashram was established by Gandhi in **1936** in a village called Segaon near Wardha in Maharashtra.
It was later renamed as Sevagram Ashram, which means the village of service. Sadaqat Ashram: This ashram was established by Gandhi in **1947** in Patna in Bihar.
It was also known as the Bihar Vidyapeeth or the Bihar University. It was a center for education and research on Gandhian philosophy and rural development.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who wrote the book "A Passage to India"?
questions.questionText: Who wrote the book "A Passage to India"?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The book "A Passage to India" was written by E. M. Forster. This novel, published in **1924**, is set against the backdrop of the British Raj and the Indian independence movement in the 1920s.
It explores the complexities of colonialism, racial tensions, and personal relationships in British India. The story follows four main characters and delves into their experiences and interactions during a trip to the Marabar Caves, which leads to a significant incident and its aftermath.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Soj-e-Vatan is the book written by:
questions.questionText: Soj-e-Vatan is the book written by:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. "Soz-e-Vatan" is a story collection written by Nawabrai, who later became well-known as Munshi Premchand.
It was published in **1908** and consisted of five stories in Urdu. The title "Soz-e-Vatan" translates to "mourning of the country" and the stories in this collection include "The Most Precious Jewel of the World," "Sheikh Makhmur," "This is My Country," "The Reward of Mourning," and "Worldly Love." The British government considered this collection seditious and confiscated and destroyed all its copies, leading Nawabrai to adopt the pen name Munshi Premchand.
**

Important Tips Notable works of Premchand: Karmabhoomi (**1932**): Set in **1930**, it explores the unity of Hindus and Muslims against British exploitation, foreshadowing India's eventual partition.
Kafan: A poignant short story depicting the irresponsibility and shamelessness of Ghisu and Madhav, who squander borrowed money meant for a shroud on drinks and food.
Gaban (**1931**): This story delves into the insatiable greed for jewels, revealing the moral and social corruption it leads to. Godan (**1936**): A realistic portrayal of rural India, addressing issues like casteism, women's exploitation, and the oppression of peasant classes.
Poos Ki Raat: Depicts a farmer's struggle to survive the harsh winter without proper clothing due to debts to moneylenders. Nirmala (**1928**): An early critique of dowry practices through the suffering of an adolescent girl married to an elderly man.
Thakur Ka Kuan: Focuses on caste-based discrimination and the denial of clean drinking water to Dalits, highlighting societal prejudice. Eidgah: A heartwarming tale of a young boy's selfless sacrifice on Eid, choosing a gift for his grandmother over sweets and toys.
Namak Ka Daroga: Tells the story of an honest salt inspector, Vanshidhar, who remains steadfast in his integrity despite the temptations of bribery.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who wrote the book 'Geographical Factors in Indian History'?
questions.questionText: Who wrote the book 'Geographical Factors in Indian History'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The book "Geographical Factors in Indian History" was written by K.M. Panikkar. It delves into the significant role that geography plays in shaping the historical evolution of a nation, influencing both internal and external relations.
The book is divided into chapters covering various aspects of geography's impact on India, including its geographical position, the role of the Himalayas, and routes of invasion.
Panikkar's work emphasizes the importance of understanding India's geographical context in the broader historical narrative.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Match the following:
